techniques.name: Figure-4 Deadfall Trap
techniques.subtitle: Heavy rock triggered by bait stick

steps.instruction: Find a flat heavy rock (5-20 lbs depending on target).
steps.helpDetail: Rock must be heavy enough to kill the animal instantly. A suffering animal will escape. Test weight by imagining it dropped on prey.

steps.instruction: Carve 3 sticks: vertical post, diagonal bait stick, horizontal trigger.
steps.helpDetail: Each stick has notches that interlock. The figure-4 shape is critical — it converts light trigger force into total collapse.

steps.instruction: Assemble under the rock: vertical stick supports horizontal trigger, diagonal holds bait.
steps.helpDetail: The bait stick angles toward the ground under the rock. When the animal pulls the bait, it releases the trigger, dropping the rock.

steps.instruction: Place bait on the end of the diagonal stick.
steps.helpDetail: Use peanut butter, berries, seeds, or meat scraps. Set on game trails. Check twice daily — trapped animals attract predators.

techniques.name: Simple Wire Snare
techniques.subtitle: Noose trap for small game

steps.instruction: Make a noose loop from wire, paracord, or strong cordage.
steps.helpDetail: Wire snares are most effective — animals can't chew through. The loop should be fist-sized for rabbits, head-sized for larger game.

steps.instruction: Secure the free end to a stake, tree, or spring pole.
steps.helpDetail: A spring pole (bent sapling) lifts the catch off the ground, preventing escape and keeping it from other predators.

steps.instruction: Set at head height on an active game trail.
steps.helpDetail: Position the bottom of the loop 2-3 inches off the ground for rabbits. Look for droppings, tracks, and worn paths.

steps.instruction: Set 10-12 snares to increase your odds.
steps.helpDetail: One snare has maybe a 10% chance. Twelve snares give you a reasonable chance of catching something. Set and forget — check morning and evening.

techniques.name: Ojibwa Bird Snare
techniques.subtitle: Perch-triggered snare for birds

steps.instruction: Drive a pole into the ground near a bird-frequented area.
steps.helpDetail: Near water sources, berry bushes, or open fields where birds land. The pole should be about 4 feet tall.

steps.instruction: Drill a hole near the top for a perch toggle.
steps.helpDetail: The toggle is a short stick inserted through the hole. It must be loose enough to release when weight is applied.

steps.instruction: Attach a snare loop to the toggle, hanging down.
steps.helpDetail: The loop lies flat on top of the perch. When a bird lands, its weight pulls the toggle, tightening the snare around its feet.

steps.instruction: Attach a weight or spring to the snare line.
steps.helpDetail: A rock weight pulling the snare tight ensures the bird can't escape. The weight also swings the bird off the perch.

techniques.name: Fish Spear (Gig)
techniques.subtitle: Pronged spear for shallow water fishing

steps.instruction: Find a straight pole 6-8 feet long.
steps.helpDetail: Green hardwood is best — dry wood splits too easily. The pole should be about 1-inch diameter.

steps.instruction: Split the tip into 4 prongs, 6-8 inches deep.
steps.helpDetail: Cross-split the end. Insert small sticks to spread the prongs apart. Sharpen each prong to a point.

steps.instruction: Harden the tips in fire.
steps.helpDetail: Rotate the tips above coals (not in flames) until they darken. This case-hardens the wood. Don't burn them.

steps.instruction: Aim BELOW the fish — refraction makes fish appear higher than they are.
steps.helpDetail: Water bends light. The fish is actually 25% deeper than it looks. Aim at its shadow on the bottom if visible.

techniques.name: Fish Basket Trap
techniques.subtitle: Passive trap that catches fish 24/7

steps.instruction: Weave a funnel-shaped basket from flexible branches or vines.
steps.helpDetail: Willow, reed, or any pliable material. The funnel narrows inward — fish swim in but can't find the exit.

steps.instruction: Create an inner funnel that points inward.
steps.helpDetail: The opening should be 3-4 inches wide at the entrance, narrowing to 1-2 inches. Fish follow the funnel in but bump against the walls trying to get out.

steps.instruction: Bait with bread, berries, or fish guts inside the trap.
steps.helpDetail: Place bait at the far end. The smell draws fish in through the funnel.

steps.instruction: Set in streams facing upstream or in tidal areas.
steps.helpDetail: Fish swim upstream naturally and enter the funnel. Weight with rocks to keep in place. Check twice daily.

techniques.name: Improvised Hook & Line
techniques.subtitle: Fishing with found materials

steps.instruction: Make hooks from thorns, bones, safety pins, or carved wood.
steps.helpDetail: Gorge hooks (straight sharp sticks) work on large fish. Bend paperclips, wire, or use strong thorns. Bone fragments can be carved into hooks.

steps.instruction: Use any strong thin material for line.
steps.helpDetail: Paracord inner strands, dental floss, thread from clothing, plant fibers twisted into cordage. Test strength before using.

steps.instruction: Bait with insects, worms, berries, or bright material.
steps.helpDetail: Turn over rocks for insects. Dig for worms. Small pieces of bright cloth or feather can serve as lures.

steps.instruction: Set multiple passive lines to maximize chance.
steps.helpDetail: Tie lines to overhanging branches with bait in the water. Check periodically. This is more efficient than active fishing.

techniques.name: Edible Insects
techniques.subtitle: The most abundant protein source on Earth

steps.instruction: Target: ants, grasshoppers, crickets, grubs, termites.
steps.helpDetail: These are safe worldwide. Avoid brightly colored insects (warning coloration), hairy caterpillars, and anything that stinks.

steps.instruction: Always cook insects if possible.
steps.helpDetail: Roasting kills parasites. Dry-roast in a pan, on a hot rock, or skewer and hold over flame. Removes legs and wings for easier eating.

steps.instruction: Remove legs, wings, and heads before eating.
steps.helpDetail: Legs have barbs that irritate the throat. Wings are indigestible. Heads can be bitter.

steps.instruction: Collect in quantity — you need volume for meaningful nutrition.
steps.helpDetail: 100g of crickets has more protein than 100g of beef. But you need to eat a LOT. Set light traps at night or shake them from vegetation in the morning when cold.

techniques.sourceName: Peterson Field Guide – Edible Wild Plants

techniques.name: Universal Edibility Test
techniques.subtitle: Test unknown plants before eating

steps.instruction: Test only ONE plant part at a time (leaf, root, fruit, stem).
steps.helpDetail: Each part may have different toxicity. Do not test more than one part simultaneously. Different parts of the same plant can range from edible to lethal.

steps.instruction: Rub on inner wrist for 15 minutes. Wait 45 minutes for reaction.
steps.helpDetail: If rash, burning, or swelling occurs — reject this plant. No reaction = proceed to next step.

steps.instruction: Touch to lip corner for 3 minutes. Then tongue tip for 15 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Any tingling, burning, or numbness = stop. Spit out and rinse mouth. Wait 15 minutes between each step.

steps.instruction: Chew a small piece for 15 minutes without swallowing.
steps.helpDetail: If no burning, bitterness, or soapy taste, swallow. Wait 8 hours monitoring for stomach pain, nausea, or diarrhea.

steps.instruction: If no ill effects after 8 hours, eat 1/4 cup. Wait another 8 hours.
steps.helpDetail: If still no effects, the plant part is likely safe. This test takes 24+ hours. Do NOT shortcut. Poisoning in a survival situation can be fatal.

techniques.name: Commonly Edible Wild Plants
techniques.subtitle: Widely distributed safe plants

steps.instruction: Dandelion: entire plant is edible — leaves, flowers, roots.
steps.helpDetail: Young leaves raw in salad, older leaves boiled to reduce bitterness. Roots roasted as coffee substitute. Grows worldwide.

steps.instruction: Cattail: the 'supermarket of the swamp.'
steps.helpDetail: Young shoots raw or cooked (taste like cucumber). Pollen as flour. Roots pounded for starch. Found at water edges worldwide.

steps.instruction: Pine: inner bark is edible, needles make vitamin C tea.
steps.helpDetail: Scrape the white/cream layer between bark and wood. Eat raw or dried and pounded into flour. Pine needle tea prevents scurvy.
steps.illustrationDescription: Pine inner bark and needle tea.

steps.instruction: Clover: flowers and leaves are edible raw or cooked.
steps.helpDetail: White and red clover are safe. High in protein for a plant. Dry and crumble for flour substitute.

steps.instruction: Plantain (broadleaf): common lawn weed, edible and medicinal.
steps.helpDetail: Young leaves raw in salads. Older leaves cooked like spinach. Crushed leaf poultice for insect bites and stings.

techniques.sourceName: Peterson Field Guide – Edible Wild Plants

techniques.name: Plants to Avoid
techniques.subtitle: Recognition of dangerous plants

steps.instruction: Avoid: white or yellow berries. 90% are poisonous.
steps.helpDetail: Exception: some white mulberries. But in a survival situation, skip ALL white/yellow berries. The risk is not worth it.

steps.instruction: Avoid: plants with milky sap (except dandelion and coconut).
steps.helpDetail: Milky sap often indicates toxic alkaloids. Even skin contact can cause blistering with some species.

steps.instruction: Avoid: umbrella-shaped flower clusters (parsley family).
steps.helpDetail: Water hemlock and poison hemlock are among the most lethal plants in North America. They look like carrots and parsley.

steps.instruction: Avoid: almond/peach scent in leaves or bark.
steps.helpDetail: This smell indicates cyanogenic glycosides — cyanide precursors. Cherry laurel, elderberry leaves, and wild almonds.

steps.instruction: Avoid: mushrooms unless you are an expert.
steps.helpDetail: Many deadly mushrooms look identical to edible ones. Amanita species kill 95% of people who eat them. No edibility test works for mushrooms. Experts only.

techniques.sourceName: Peterson Field Guide – Edible Wild Plants

techniques.name: Cooking Over Open Fire
techniques.subtitle: Methods for cooking without pots

steps.instruction: Spit roasting: skewer meat on a green stick over coals.
steps.helpDetail: Use COALS, not flames. Flames char the outside while leaving the inside raw. Keep 6-8 inches above coals. Turn frequently.

steps.instruction: Rock cooking: heat flat rocks and cook directly on them.
steps.helpDetail: Use dry, non-porous rocks. WET or RIVER rocks can EXPLODE when heated — they contain trapped moisture. Heat gradually.

steps.instruction: Clay baking: coat meat in thick clay and bake in coals.
steps.helpDetail: The clay seals in moisture and creates an oven. When cracked open, skin/feathers peel off with the clay. Works great for birds and fish.

steps.instruction: Leaf wrapping: wrap food in large non-toxic leaves and place on coals.
steps.helpDetail: Banana leaves, burdock, grape leaves. Creates a steaming packet. Food cooks evenly and stays moist.

techniques.name: Stone Boiling
techniques.subtitle: Boil water in non-fireproof containers

steps.instruction: Fill a bark container, skin bag, or hollowed log with water.
steps.helpDetail: These containers can't go on fire but can hold boiling water. Even a plastic bag works if filled with water (the water prevents it from melting).

steps.instruction: Heat rocks in a fire until glowing hot.
steps.helpDetail: Use DRY rocks only. River rocks or wet rocks can shatter explosively. Heat for 20-30 minutes until glowing.

steps.instruction: Transfer hot rocks to the water with sticks or tongs.
steps.helpDetail: Brush ash off rocks before transferring. Drop carefully to avoid splashing boiling water.

steps.instruction: Continue adding rocks until water reaches a rolling boil.
steps.helpDetail: 2-3 fist-sized rocks will boil 1 liter. Remove cooled rocks with sticks and add newly heated ones to maintain the boil.

techniques.name: Meat Preservation (Smoking/Drying)
techniques.subtitle: Make meat last for weeks

steps.instruction: Cut meat into thin strips — 1/4 inch thick.
steps.helpDetail: Cut WITH the grain for jerky. Thinner strips dry faster and spoil less. Remove all fat — fat goes rancid.

steps.instruction: For smoking: build a low smoky fire in an enclosed space.
steps.helpDetail: Use green wood or wet wood to create dense smoke. Build a tripod or rack 2-3 feet above the fire. Smoke for 6-12 hours.

steps.instruction: For drying: hang strips in direct sun and wind.
steps.helpDetail: Hot, dry, windy conditions are ideal. Hang on a line or rack. Protect from flies with thin cloth. Sun-dry for 1-2 days.

steps.instruction: Meat is done when it cracks but doesn't break when bent.
steps.helpDetail: Properly dried meat can last 2-4 weeks without refrigeration. Store in breathable cloth or bark containers. Mold means it's too moist.

techniques.name: Pemmican
techniques.subtitle: High-calorie survival super food

steps.instruction: Pound dried meat into powder.
steps.helpDetail: Use rocks as mortar and pestle. Meat should be completely dry — any moisture causes spoilage. Pound until fibrous powder.

steps.instruction: Mix meat powder with rendered fat (50/50 ratio).
steps.helpDetail: Melt animal fat slowly. Strain out solids. Mix warm liquid fat with meat powder until it holds together.

steps.instruction: Add dried berries or nuts if available.
steps.helpDetail: Crushed dried berries add vitamins and flavor. Nuts add additional fat and calories. These are optional.

steps.instruction: Form into bars or balls and let cool.
steps.helpDetail: Pemmican stores for months at room temperature and years if kept cool. One pound provides 3,500 calories — a full day's rations.

techniques.name: Catching Crayfish
techniques.subtitle: Freshwater lobsters found in most streams

steps.instruction: Look under rocks in shallow streams and rivers.
steps.helpDetail: Crayfish hide under rocks during the day. Grab behind the head to avoid pincers.

steps.instruction: Bait trap: container with small opening and bait inside.
steps.helpDetail: Cut plastic bottle with top inverted makes a trap. Bait with fish guts or bread.

steps.instruction: Boil 5-7 minutes until bright red. Eat tail meat.
steps.helpDetail: Never eat raw—parasites. Discard any dead before cooking.

techniques.name: Edible Seaweed Harvesting
techniques.subtitle: The ocean's most nutritious free food

steps.instruction: Harvest seaweed attached to rocks, not floating loose.
steps.helpDetail: Floating seaweed may be decomposing. Most attached seaweed is edible.

steps.instruction: Rinse in fresh water if available to reduce salt.
steps.helpDetail: High salt worsens dehydration. Eat in small amounts if no fresh water.

steps.instruction: Eat raw, dried, or add to soups. Rich in iodine and vitamins.
steps.helpDetail: Kelp, nori, dulse, sea lettuce all edible. Sun-dry for preservation.

techniques.sourceName: Peterson Field Guide – Edible Wild Plants

techniques.name: Cattail Harvesting
techniques.subtitle: Survival supermarket — every part is edible

steps.instruction: Identify: tall reeds with brown cigar-shaped seed heads.
steps.helpDetail: Found in marshes, ponds, wet areas worldwide. One of safest wild foods.

steps.instruction: Root: dig rhizome, peel and eat raw or roast. Starchy.
steps.helpDetail: Potato-like. Can be dried and ground into flour. Available year-round.

steps.instruction: Young shoots: peel outer leaves, inner core is edible raw.
steps.helpDetail: Called Cossack asparagus. Crisp, mild flavor. Spring harvest.

steps.instruction: Pollen: shake from flower head. High-protein flour substitute.
steps.helpDetail: Mix with water for pancakes. Summer harvest only.

techniques.name: Pine Needle Tea
techniques.subtitle: Vitamin C from any pine tree

steps.instruction: Gather green pine needles. Avoid yew (flat needles, red berries—toxic).
steps.helpDetail: True pines with needles in bundles of 2-5 are safe.

steps.instruction: Chop or bruise needles to release oils.
steps.helpDetail: Cut into small pieces or crush with rock.

steps.instruction: Steep in hot (not boiling) water for 10-15 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Boiling destroys vitamin C. Strain before drinking.

steps.instruction: 5x more vitamin C than orange juice. Prevents scurvy.
steps.helpDetail: Also contains vitamin A. Pleasant forest taste.

techniques.name: Smoking Meat for Preservation
techniques.subtitle: Preserve meat for weeks without refrigeration

steps.instruction: Cut meat into thin strips — 1/4 inch maximum.
steps.helpDetail: Thinner strips smoke faster and more evenly. Cut against grain.

steps.instruction: Build smoking rack 2-3 feet above small, smoky fire.
steps.helpDetail: Green wood tripod with cross-sticks. Smoke, not flame.

steps.instruction: Use hardwood: oak, hickory, maple, apple. Never pine.
steps.helpDetail: Resin from pine makes meat toxic. Green hardwood = most smoke.

steps.instruction: Smoke 6-12 hours until dry and dark. Should snap, not fold.
steps.helpDetail: Properly smoked jerky lasts 2-4 weeks unrefrigerated.

techniques.name: Animal Skinning & Butchering
techniques.subtitle: Field dress game safely

steps.instruction: Shallow cut from pelvis to ribcage — do not puncture organs.
steps.helpDetail: Use knife tip only. Lift skin, cut outward. Organ puncture contaminates meat.

steps.instruction: Remove organs in one mass. Save heart, liver, kidneys.
steps.helpDetail: Cut windpipe and esophagus at top. These organs are nutrient-dense.

steps.instruction: Skin by pulling hide away, cutting connective tissue.
steps.helpDetail: Start at legs, work toward spine. Keep blade against hide.

steps.instruction: Separate meat along muscle groups. Cook or preserve immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Hindquarters = most meat. Backstrap along spine = best cut.

techniques.name: Shellfish & Mussel Gathering
techniques.subtitle: Tidal zone protein available twice daily

steps.instruction: Gather at low tide. Mussels on rocks, clams in sand.
steps.helpDetail: Look for small holes that squirt water for clams.

steps.instruction: Discard any that are open and don't close when tapped.
steps.helpDetail: Open shells = dead = bacterial contamination.

steps.instruction: Boil until shells open, 5-10 minutes. Discard unopened.
steps.helpDetail: Raw shellfish carry parasites and bacteria.

steps.instruction: Avoid during red tide or warm months in tropics.
steps.helpDetail: Paralytic shellfish poisoning is fatal. No field treatment.

techniques.name: Wild Onion & Garlic ID
techniques.subtitle: Smells like onion = safe. No smell = death camas

steps.instruction: Crush leaf or bulb. MUST smell of onion or garlic.
steps.helpDetail: Death camas looks identical but has NO onion smell. Fatal if eaten.

steps.instruction: Check for onion-like bulb with papery skin.
steps.helpDetail: Layered bulbs like store-bought. Round with dry outer layers.

steps.instruction: Leaves should be grass-like, from the base.
steps.helpDetail: Long, flat or tubular. All from ground level.

steps.instruction: Eat raw, roasted, or in soups. Entire plant edible.
steps.helpDetail: Bulb, leaves, flowers all edible. Roasting sweetens.

techniques.name: Frog Catching
techniques.subtitle: Night hunting with a light source

steps.instruction: Hunt at night near water with light. Frogs freeze in light.
steps.helpDetail: Walk along stream banks and pond edges. Eyes reflect light.

steps.instruction: Grab quickly from behind. They are fast and slippery.
steps.helpDetail: Approach slowly, grab with fast swoop. Pin to ground.

steps.instruction: Avoid brightly colored frogs — many are toxic.
steps.helpDetail: Red, blue, yellow, orange = poison. Stick to green/brown.

steps.instruction: Remove skin, cook legs thoroughly. Discard organs.
steps.helpDetail: Skin may have irritants. Legs are the meat. Taste like chicken.

techniques.name: Pine Bark Flour
techniques.subtitle: Emergency starch from inner pine bark

steps.instruction: Strip the outer bark from a pine tree to expose the inner bark.
steps.helpDetail: The cambium layer — thin, moist, whitish layer between the rough outer bark and the wood. Peel in vertical strips. Take from multiple trees to avoid killing any one tree.

steps.instruction: Scrape off the inner bark (cambium) in thin strips.
steps.helpDetail: Use a knife edge to scrape. You want the soft, moist inner bark, not the hard outer bark or the wood underneath. It tastes slightly sweet and resinous.

steps.instruction: Dry the strips and grind into flour. Mix with water for porridge.
steps.helpDetail: Dried pine bark flour contains 500-600 calories per 100g. It can be eaten raw, boiled into porridge, or mixed with other flours. It kept Scandinavian populations alive through famine.

techniques.name: Crayfish Trapping
techniques.subtitle: Easy protein from streams and ponds

steps.instruction: Look for crayfish in shallow, rocky streams and pond edges.
steps.helpDetail: Turn over rocks — crayfish hide underneath during the day. Active at night. Found on every continent except Antarctica.

steps.instruction: Build a simple funnel trap from sticks or a plastic bottle.
steps.helpDetail: Cut a bottle in half, invert the top into the bottom to create a funnel entrance. Or weave a cone from flexible sticks.

steps.instruction: Bait with fish scraps, meat, or strong-smelling food. Place in shallow water.
steps.helpDetail: Crayfish are scavengers attracted to strong smells. Anchor the trap with rocks so it doesn't drift. Set in the evening for best results.

steps.instruction: Boil crayfish in water for 5 minutes. Eat the tail meat.
steps.helpDetail: MUST be cooked — crayfish carry parasites when raw. Twist the tail from the body, peel the shell, eat the meat.

techniques.name: Ojibwe Bird Snare
techniques.subtitle: Traditional bird snare using a bent sapling

steps.instruction: Find a flexible sapling near bird feeding areas.
steps.helpDetail: Look for areas where birds feed on the ground — berry bushes, seed heads, water edges.

steps.instruction: Bend the sapling down and secure it with a trigger mechanism.
steps.helpDetail: Carve a simple figure-4 trigger or use a toggle and notch system. The trigger holds the sapling down under tension.

steps.instruction: Attach a snare loop made from cordage to the trigger.
steps.helpDetail: The snare loop should be slightly larger than the bird's foot. Lay it flat around bait.

steps.instruction: Place bait (berries, seeds, insects) around the trigger.
steps.helpDetail: Set multiple snares in a line for better odds. Check every few hours.

techniques.name: Pemmican Making
techniques.subtitle: The ultimate survival food: meat, fat, and berries

steps.instruction: Dry lean meat into jerky, then pound into powder.
steps.helpDetail: Any lean meat works. Slice thin and dry completely over smoke or in the sun. Once bone-dry, pound between rocks until it's a fine, fluffy powder.

steps.instruction: Render fat by slowly heating it until it's liquid.
steps.helpDetail: Cut fat into small pieces, heat slowly. Strain the liquid fat through cloth. Pure rendered fat stores for months.

steps.instruction: Mix powdered meat with warm rendered fat at a 1:1 ratio.
steps.helpDetail: The fat should be warm but not hot. Mix thoroughly. Optionally add dried, crushed berries for nutrition and flavor.

steps.instruction: Press into bars. Stores for months without refrigeration.
steps.helpDetail: Pemmican is the most calorie-dense survival food: 3,000+ calories per pound. Keeps for years if stored dry.

techniques.name: Jerky Without Salt
techniques.subtitle: Preserve meat when you have no salt

steps.instruction: Cut meat into thin strips (1/4 inch) along the grain.
steps.helpDetail: Thinner strips dry faster. Remove ALL fat — fat does not dry, it goes rancid. Lean meat only.

steps.instruction: Build a drying rack over a smoky fire.
steps.helpDetail: Use green wood for more smoke (hickory, oak, mesquite). The smoke is antimicrobial and adds flavor.

steps.instruction: Hang strips on the rack 6-12 inches above the smoke.
steps.helpDetail: Don't let strips touch each other. Air must circulate. Flip every few hours.

steps.instruction: Dry until strips crack when bent but don't break.
steps.helpDetail: Takes 6-12 hours over smoke. Without salt, keeps 1-2 weeks. Eat it fast or make pemmican.

techniques.name: Mushroom Safety Rules
techniques.subtitle: Critical rules for mushroom identification and consumption

steps.instruction: NEVER eat a mushroom you cannot 100% identify. No exceptions.
steps.helpDetail: Many deadly mushrooms look almost identical to edible ones. Death cap (Amanita phalloides) kills more people than any other mushroom and initially looks like several edible species. There is NO universal test for edibility.

steps.instruction: Learn and focus ONLY on a few easily-identified safe species.
steps.helpDetail: Beginner-safe species with few lookalikes: chicken of the woods (orange shelf mushroom), giant puffball (must be pure white inside), morels (honeycomb pattern). Learn these from an expert, not a book alone.

steps.instruction: When in doubt, skip mushrooms. The caloric value isn't worth the risk.
steps.helpDetail: Most mushrooms are low in calories (20-30 cal per cup). A misidentification can cause liver failure and death. The risk/reward ratio is terrible. Focus caloric foraging on plants, insects, and animals instead.

techniques.name: Seaweed & Kelp Harvesting
techniques.subtitle: Harvest nutritious seaweed from coastal environments

steps.instruction: Harvest seaweed from clean water, attached to rocks (not free-floating).
steps.helpDetail: Free-floating seaweed may be decomposing. Harvest from rocks in clean, moving water. Avoid areas near sewage outlets, harbors, or industrial sites. Best at low tide when more is exposed.

steps.instruction: Rinse thoroughly and dry in sun or near fire to preserve.
steps.helpDetail: Fresh seaweed spoils within 24 hours. Dried seaweed lasts months. Spread thin on rocks or hang on cord in sun. Can also eat fresh — most edible seaweed can be eaten raw or lightly boiled.

steps.instruction: Avoid blue-green algae (cyanobacteria) — it's toxic.
steps.helpDetail: Blue-green algae looks like green paint in water. It produces neurotoxins and liver toxins. Real seaweed is brown, green, or red and has a plant-like structure. If it looks like scum or slime, avoid it.

techniques.name: Acorn Processing & Cooking
techniques.subtitle: Leach tannins from acorns to make edible flour

steps.instruction: Collect fallen acorns. Discard any with holes (insect larvae inside).
steps.helpDetail: White oak acorns are less bitter than red oak. All acorns are edible after processing. Crack shells and remove nutmeat. Brown, intact nutmeat is good. Black or moldy = discard.

steps.instruction: Crush nutmeat and soak in running water or change water repeatedly.
steps.helpDetail: Tannins make raw acorns bitter and cause stomach problems. Leaching methods: place crushed acorn in mesh bag in running stream for 24-48 hours, OR boil in multiple changes of water (5-7 changes until water runs clear).

steps.instruction: Dry leached acorn meal and grind into flour for cooking.
steps.helpDetail: Dried acorn flour can be used for flatbread, porridge, or thickening. 100g of acorn flour = 500+ calories. Acorns were a staple food for indigenous peoples worldwide. An oak tree produces 10,000-20,000 acorns per year.

techniques.name: Bird Snare Construction
techniques.subtitle: Build simple snares for catching birds

steps.instruction: Build an Ojibwa bird pole: tall pole with perch and snare.
steps.helpDetail: Drill a hole near top of a 6-foot pole. Insert a short perch stick. Tie a small noose of cordage around the perch. When a bird lands, its weight pulls the perch, tightening the noose around its feet.

steps.instruction: Place in open area near water or food sources where birds congregate.
steps.helpDetail: Birds prefer the highest perch available. Place your pole taller than surrounding vegetation. Near water is ideal — birds landing to drink will use your perch. Set multiple poles for better odds.

steps.instruction: Check snares frequently. Process caught birds immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Pluck feathers while still warm (easier). Gut immediately. Small birds can be roasted whole on a spit. All birds are edible — there are no poisonous birds (though some taste terrible). Cook thoroughly for food safety.

techniques.name: Small Game Field Dressing
techniques.subtitle: Clean and prepare small animals for cooking

steps.instruction: Make a small cut through the belly skin (avoid puncturing organs).
steps.helpDetail: Pinch belly skin and make a small cut. Insert two fingers under the skin to lift it away from organs, then cut between your fingers. Puncturing the intestines contaminates the meat.

steps.instruction: Remove intestines, stomach, and bladder. Keep liver, heart, and kidneys.
steps.helpDetail: Reach in and pull organs out in one mass. Heart, liver, and kidneys are the most nutritious parts — eat them first. Liver is extremely nutrient-dense. Check liver for spots (parasites) — discard if spotted.

steps.instruction: Skin by pulling hide off like removing a sock.
steps.helpDetail: For rabbits: cut around all four feet. Cut from foot to belly cut on each leg. Pull skin off like peeling a banana. Rinse carcass with clean water. Cook thoroughly — all wild game must be fully cooked.

techniques.name: Fish Spearing
techniques.subtitle: Improvise a spear and catch fish in shallow water

steps.instruction: Create a multi-pronged spear by splitting a sapling tip into 4 prongs.
steps.helpDetail: Choose a straight pole 6-8 feet long. Split the tip into 4 equal sections using your knife. Wedge a small stone between the prongs to spread them. Sharpen each prong. Bind below the split to prevent further splitting.

steps.instruction: Stand still in shallow water. Wait for fish to come to you.
steps.helpDetail: Patience is essential. Stand downstream and remain motionless. Fish will approach within minutes. Muddy your feet's scent by rubbing with mud before entering. Dawn and dusk are best times.

steps.instruction: Aim below the fish to compensate for water refraction.
steps.helpDetail: Light bends at the water surface, making the fish appear higher than it actually is. Aim at the bottom of the fish's shadow, not the fish itself. Thrust quickly and pin against the bottom.

techniques.name: Finding & Eating Grubs
techniques.subtitle: High-calorie protein from rotting wood

steps.instruction: Look inside rotting logs and under bark for beetle larvae (grubs).
steps.helpDetail: Break apart soft, rotting wood. White or cream-colored larvae are beetle grubs. They're common in dead trees worldwide. Also check under rocks and in soil near rotting vegetation.

steps.instruction: Squeeze grubs gently to expel gut contents (optional but improves taste).
steps.helpDetail: Pinch behind the head and squeeze toward the tail to expel digestive contents. This removes bitter flavors. Not strictly necessary for safety — just preference.

steps.instruction: Roast on a stick near fire, or skewer and fry. Cook thoroughly.
steps.helpDetail: Roasted grubs taste nutty and buttery. A single large grub can contain 50+ calories — mostly fat and protein. 10 large grubs = a small meal. Can also be dried for storage.

techniques.name: Bone Broth for Maximum Nutrition
techniques.subtitle: Extract every calorie and mineral from animal bones

steps.instruction: Crack or smash bones to expose marrow before boiling.
steps.helpDetail: Marrow is the most calorie-dense part of an animal (80% fat). Use rocks to crack long bones. Marrow can also be roasted and eaten directly — push it out of the split bone with a stick.

steps.instruction: Boil cracked bones in water for 4-12 hours, keeping water level up.
steps.helpDetail: Long boiling extracts calcium, phosphorus, magnesium, and collagen from bones. The longer you boil, the more nutrition extracted. If possible, add acidic ingredients (wild berries) to extract more minerals.

steps.instruction: Drink broth hot. Skim fat from surface for cooking or calories.
steps.helpDetail: The fat floating on top is pure energy. Save it for cooking other foods. The broth provides minerals, electrolytes, and gelatin. Bones can be reboiled 2-3 times for diminishing but real returns.

techniques.name: Wild Garlic & Onion Identification
techniques.subtitle: Find wild alliums for nutrition and flavor

steps.instruction: Look for grass-like leaves with a strong garlic or onion smell when crushed.
steps.helpDetail: Wild garlic (ramps), wild onion, and wild leeks all have edible bulbs and leaves. The SMELL is the key identifier. If it doesn't smell like garlic or onion, DO NOT eat it — similar-looking plants (death camas) are deadly.

steps.instruction: Dig up the bulb. It should look like a small onion or garlic clove.
steps.helpDetail: The bulb should have layers (like an onion) or be solid (like garlic). If the bulb has no onion smell, it is NOT wild allium. Death camas has a similar leaf but no allium smell and its bulb has no layers.

steps.instruction: Eat raw, add to cooking, or dry for preservation.
steps.helpDetail: All parts are edible: bulbs, leaves, flowers. Excellent addition to bland survival food. Contains vitamin C, selenium, and has antimicrobial properties. Dry and crush leaves for seasoning that stores well.

techniques.name: Dandelion: Complete Edible Plant
techniques.subtitle: Every part of the dandelion is edible and nutritious

steps.instruction: Identify: jagged leaves, hollow stem with milky sap, yellow flower.
steps.helpDetail: Dandelions are found on every continent. Leaves form a rosette at ground level. Stem is hollow and produces white latex when broken. Flower is a single composite yellow head. No poisonous lookalikes.

steps.instruction: Harvest leaves (youngest are least bitter), flowers, and roots.
steps.helpDetail: Young leaves: eat raw in salads or boil to reduce bitterness. Flowers: eat raw or fry (remove green parts — they're bitter). Roots: roast until dark and grind for coffee substitute, or boil as root vegetable.

steps.instruction: Rich in vitamins A, C, K, and minerals. More nutritious than spinach.
steps.helpDetail: Dandelion greens contain more vitamin A than carrots, more iron than spinach, and more calcium than milk per gram. Harvest away from roads (lead contamination) and lawns (herbicides).

techniques.sourceName: Peterson Field Guide – Edible Wild Plants

techniques.name: Clay Pot Construction & Cooking
techniques.subtitle: Make a cooking vessel from natural clay

steps.instruction: Find clay near stream banks — it holds shape when squeezed and doesn't crumble.
steps.helpDetail: Good clay is smooth, sticky when wet, and holds its form. Test: roll a pencil-thin coil. If it bends without cracking, the clay is suitable. Remove stones and roots. Knead thoroughly to remove air bubbles.

steps.instruction: Build pot using coil method: roll long snakes of clay and stack in circles.
steps.helpDetail: Roll clay into ropes the thickness of your thumb. Coil upward from a flat base, smoothing each coil into the one below. Keep walls even thickness (1/2 inch). Smooth inside and out. Make walls thicker at the base.

steps.instruction: Dry slowly for 24-48 hours, then fire in a hot campfire for 2-3 hours.
steps.helpDetail: Fast drying causes cracking. Place near fire for gentle drying first. Then bury in hot coals for 2-3 hours. Let cool slowly in the ashes. The pot will be fragile but functional for boiling water and cooking stews.

techniques.name: Earth Oven (Ground Pit Cooking)
techniques.subtitle: Cook large quantities of food using heated stones underground

steps.instruction: Dig a pit 2-3 feet deep and wide. Build a large fire in it with stones.
steps.helpDetail: Use dense, non-porous stones (river rocks can explode from trapped water). Burn a hot fire for 2-3 hours until stones are glowing. Scrape out coals but leave hot stones in the pit.

steps.instruction: Layer: hot stones, wet leaves/grass, food, wet leaves/grass, more hot stones.
steps.helpDetail: The wet vegetation creates steam for cooking. Wrap food in large green leaves to protect from ash. Layer food that takes longest on the bottom (root vegetables) and fastest on top (greens, fish).

steps.instruction: Cover with earth and let cook 4-8 hours. The earth insulates the heat.
steps.helpDetail: Seal edges with packed dirt so steam can't escape. This method slow-cooks everything evenly. Root vegetables become soft and sweet. Meat falls off the bone. Can cook for a large group efficiently.

techniques.name: Emergency Rationing Strategy
techniques.subtitle: How to eat when food is scarce

steps.instruction: Inventory EVERYTHING edible immediately.
steps.helpDetail: Count every bar, cracker, and candy. Divide total calories by days until rescue (estimated). Goal: 500+ cal/day.

steps.instruction: Do NOT eat for the first 24 hours.
steps.helpDetail: Your body has reserves. Eating triggers digestion which uses water. Save food for when hunger pangs stabilize.

steps.instruction: Eat small amounts during activity peaks.
steps.helpDetail: Eat before heavy exertion (hiking/building) or before sleep (to stay warm). Don't graze all day.

techniques.name: Wild Fruit Identification
techniques.subtitle: Identify safe edible wild fruits

steps.instruction: Use the Universal Edibility Test: test ONE plant part at a time.
steps.helpDetail: Never eat unknown plants without testing. Test only one plant and one part (fruit, leaf, stem) at a time. If you react to any part, discard the entire plant.

steps.instruction: Avoid: white/yellow berries, umbrella-shaped flowers, milky sap.
steps.helpDetail: General rule: 90% of white and yellow berries are poisonous. Plants with milky sap or umbrella flower clusters (like poison hemlock) are dangerous. Red berries are 50/50.

steps.instruction: Safe bets: blackberries, raspberries, blueberries, rose hips.
steps.helpDetail: These are widely distributed and easy to identify. Blackberries/raspberries have compound berries on bramble bushes. Rose hips are the red fruit below rose flowers.

steps.instruction: Smell test: crush a small amount and smell. Reject if bitter or almond-like.
steps.helpDetail: Bitter almond smell indicates cyanide compounds. Soapy smell indicates saponins. Both are toxic. A pleasant, fruity smell is a good sign but not conclusive.

steps.instruction: Skin test: rub on inner wrist, wait 15 minutes for reaction.
steps.helpDetail: If no rash, burning, or numbness, place a small amount on your lip. Wait 15 minutes. Then on tongue. Wait 15 minutes. Then chew and spit. If no reaction after 8 hours, eat a small amount.

techniques.name: Pine Cambium Harvest
techniques.subtitle: Eat the inner bark of pine trees

steps.instruction: Select a living pine tree. Cut through the outer bark to expose the creamy-white inner bark (cambium).
steps.helpDetail: The cambium is the thin layer between the rough outer bark and the wood. It is most nutritious in spring. All pine species are edible.
steps.illustrationDescription: Select a living pine tree. Cut

steps.instruction: Peel strips of cambium using a knife blade, scraping along the wood grain.
steps.helpDetail: The strips peel like ribbons. Harvest from fallen or already-damaged trees when possible to avoid killing living trees.
steps.illustrationDescription: Peel strips of cambium using a

steps.instruction: Eat raw — it is chewy with a mild, slightly resinous flavor.
steps.helpDetail: Cambium can be eaten as-is for an emergency calorie source. It contains starch, sugars, vitamins, and fiber. Not delicious, but sustaining.
steps.illustrationDescription: Eat raw — it is chewy with a m

steps.instruction: For better flavor and nutrition: dry strips and grind into flour for flatbread.
steps.helpDetail: Dried cambium flour can be mixed with other wild foods or used alone. Mix with water and cook on a hot rock for a simple bread.

steps.instruction: Alternatively: boil strips into a starchy noodle-like food or fry in rendered animal fat.
steps.helpDetail: Boiling softens the fibers and makes the starch more digestible. A filling meal can be made from cambium alone in an emergency.

techniques.name: Clover Identification & Use
techniques.subtitle: Identify and eat common clover species

steps.instruction: Identify clover: three round leaflets with a pale V-shaped chevron on each. Grows in fields, lawns, and meadows.
steps.helpDetail: White clover (Trifolium repens) and red clover (Trifolium pratense) are both edible and widespread across temperate regions worldwide.

steps.instruction: All parts are edible: leaves, flowers, stems, and roots.
steps.helpDetail: Leaves are best raw when young and tender. Older leaves become tough. Flowers are sweet — especially red clover blossoms.

steps.instruction: Eat flowers raw as a sweet trail snack, or steep in hot water for a mild, slightly sweet tea.
steps.helpDetail: Clover tea provides vitamin C and has mild medicinal properties. Steep fresh or dried flower heads for 10 minutes.
steps.illustrationDescription: Eat flowers raw as a sweet tra

steps.instruction: Cook leaves by boiling for 5-10 minutes to reduce the mild bitterness.
steps.helpDetail: Boiling also makes the nutrients more bioavailable. Treat like spinach — add to soups, stews, or eat as a hot green vegetable.

steps.instruction: Dry flowers and leaves for long-term storage. Dried clover retains nutrition for months.
steps.helpDetail: Spread flowers and leaves on a warm rock in the sun. Once crumbly-dry, store in a bark container. Can be ground into flour supplement.
steps.illustrationDescription: Dry flowers and leaves for lon

techniques.sourceName: Peterson Field Guide – Edible Wild Plants

techniques.name: Rose Hip Tea
techniques.subtitle: Brew vitamin C-rich tea from wild roses

steps.instruction: Identify rose hips: red-orange fruit at the base of wild rose flowers, appearing in late summer through winter.
steps.helpDetail: All wild rose species produce edible hips. They cling to the bush after leaves fall, making them easy to harvest in autumn and winter.

steps.instruction: Harvest hips when red and slightly soft. They sweeten after the first frost.
steps.helpDetail: Hard, green hips are too astringent. Overripe hips (dark red, mushy) are fine for tea. Avoid any with mold.
steps.illustrationDescription: Harvest hips when red and slig

steps.instruction: Split hips open and remove the seeds and fine hairs inside. The hairs are irritating if swallowed.
steps.helpDetail: The interior hairs can cause throat irritation. For tea this matters less since you'll strain the liquid, but remove when eating raw.
steps.illustrationDescription: Split hips open and remove the

steps.instruction: Crush or chop the hip flesh. Steep in hot (not boiling) water for 15-20 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Boiling water destroys vitamin C. Heat water until steaming but not rolling boiling. Use 4-5 hips per cup.
steps.illustrationDescription: Crush or chop the hip flesh. S

steps.instruction: Rose hips contain 20x more vitamin C than oranges by weight. Critical for preventing scurvy in long-term survival.
steps.helpDetail: A daily cup of rose hip tea provides adequate vitamin C. The tea also contains vitamins A, E, and K. Tastes slightly tart and fruity.

techniques.sourceName: Peterson Field Guide – Edible Wild Plants

techniques.name: Organ Meat Nutrition
techniques.subtitle: Maximize nutrition from hunted game organs

steps.instruction: Liver: the most nutrient-dense organ. Provides massive amounts of vitamins A, B12, iron, and protein.
steps.helpDetail: Eat liver within 24 hours — it spoils faster than muscle meat. All mammalian and bird livers are edible. Do NOT eat polar bear liver (toxic vitamin A levels).

steps.instruction: Heart: lean muscle tissue, high in protein, iron, and CoQ10. Treat like a steak — slice and cook.
steps.helpDetail: The heart is the cleanest organ — pure muscle with no toxins. Slice thin and cook over flame. Best eating quality of any organ.

steps.instruction: Kidneys: high in B12 and selenium. Soak in cold water for 30 minutes first to reduce strong flavor.
steps.helpDetail: Cut kidneys in half, remove the white fat core, and soak. Then cook thoroughly over fire. Nutritious but acquired taste.
steps.illustrationDescription: Kidneys: high in B12 and selen

steps.instruction: Stomach and intestines: clean thoroughly, boil for 30+ minutes. Good source of fat and protein.
steps.helpDetail: Clean by turning inside-out and washing. The stomach lining (tripe) is edible. Intestines can be used as sausage casings for preserving meat.

steps.instruction: Bone marrow: crack long bones and roast near fire. Marrow is calorie-dense and rich in healthy fats.
steps.helpDetail: A single deer femur provides 100+ calories of pure fat. Roast bones upright near coals, then crack open when sizzling. Marrow scoops out with a stick.

techniques.name: Gill Net Fishing
techniques.subtitle: Set a passive net to catch fish while you sleep

steps.instruction: Construct a net from paracord inner strands, fishing line, natural cordage, or unraveled fabric.
steps.helpDetail: Mesh size determines catch size: 1-inch mesh catches small fish (pan-sized), 3-inch mesh catches larger fish. Choose based on water body.

steps.instruction: Tie mesh using a series of square knots or sheet bends. Create a net 3-4 feet tall and as wide as the stream.
steps.helpDetail: Use a gauge stick to keep mesh size consistent. Tie loops at top and bottom for attachment. Net making is tedious but the net works 24/7.
steps.illustrationDescription: Tie mesh using a series of squ

steps.instruction: Attach floats (bark, foam, sealed containers) to the top line and weights (rocks) to the bottom.
steps.helpDetail: Float the top line on the surface. Weight the bottom to the stream bed. The net hangs vertically like a curtain in the water.

steps.instruction: Set the net across a fish travel lane: stream narrows, inlet/outlet of a pool, near structure.
steps.helpDetail: Fish follow current edges, structure lines, and depth transitions. Set at dawn or dusk when fish are most active.
steps.illustrationDescription: Set the net across a fish trav

steps.instruction: Check the net every few hours. Remove fish promptly — dead fish decay fast and deter other catches.
steps.helpDetail: A gill net catches fish passively 24/7. In a survival situation, this is the most efficient food-gathering method available.
steps.illustrationDescription: Check the net every few hours.

techniques.name: Trotline Fishing
techniques.subtitle: Set multiple hooks on one line across water

steps.instruction: Tie a strong main line between two fixed points across a stream or pond.
steps.helpDetail: Trees, stakes, or heavy rocks on each bank work. The line should be taut enough to not sag underwater but loose enough to absorb fish strikes.
steps.illustrationDescription: Tie a strong main line between

steps.instruction: Attach dropper lines (12-18 inches) every 3-4 feet along the main line.
steps.helpDetail: Use lighter line for droppers so a snagged hook breaks free without losing the main trotline. Tie with a clinch knot or loop knot.

steps.instruction: Bait each hook — use insects, worms, small fish, bread, or meat scraps.
steps.helpDetail: Vary bait types to determine what fish are eating. Live bait outperforms dead bait. Insect larvae from under rocks are excellent.

steps.instruction: Set the trotline so hooks hang at different depths — some near surface, some mid-water, some near bottom.
steps.helpDetail: Different fish species feed at different depths. Varying hook depth maximizes your catch diversity and chances.
steps.illustrationDescription: Set the trotline so hooks hang

steps.instruction: Check every 2-4 hours. Re-bait empty hooks. Process caught fish immediately.
steps.helpDetail: A 10-hook trotline multiplies your fishing effort by 10. It works while you do other survival tasks. Reset before dark for overnight catch.

techniques.name: Wild Plantain (Plantago)
techniques.subtitle: Identify and eat this common weed

steps.instruction: Identify: rosette of broad oval leaves with prominent parallel veins, growing close to the ground.
steps.helpDetail: Common plantain (Plantago major) grows worldwide in disturbed soil — roadsides, paths, lawns. NOT the banana-like tropical plantain.

steps.instruction: Young leaves (under 4 inches) are best raw — mild flavor, slightly bitter like spinach.
steps.helpDetail: As leaves grow larger they become tough and stringy. Pick the youngest, smallest leaves from the center of the rosette.

steps.instruction: Cook larger leaves by boiling for 5-10 minutes. The parallel veins become stringy — remove them like celery strings.
steps.helpDetail: Pull the thick veins out after cooking. The remaining leaf tissue is tender and nutritious. Add to any wild stew.

steps.instruction: Seeds: harvest the rat-tail seed spikes when brown. Rub to free seeds. Use as grain substitute.
steps.helpDetail: Plantain seeds are tiny but nutritious. Grind between rocks for flour, or boil into porridge. They contain mucilage that thickens soups.

steps.instruction: Medicinal: crush fresh leaves and apply as a poultice to insect bites, stings, and minor wounds.
steps.helpDetail: Plantain leaf poultice draws out toxins and reduces inflammation. It has been used medicinally for thousands of years across cultures.

techniques.sourceName: Peterson Field Guide – Edible Wild Plants

techniques.name: Acorn Leaching Methods
techniques.subtitle: Remove bitter tannins from acorns for eating

steps.instruction: Crack acorn shells with a rock. Remove meats and discard any with insect holes or dark spots.
steps.helpDetail: White oak acorns have the least tannin and shortest leaching time. Red/black oak acorns require more leaching. Both become edible.
steps.illustrationDescription: Crack acorn shells with a rock

steps.instruction: Cold water method (best): crush meats, submerge in cold flowing water (stream) for 24-48 hours.
steps.helpDetail: Place crushed acorns in a mesh bag and anchor in a flowing stream. The water gradually dissolves and carries away the tannins.

steps.instruction: Hot water method (faster): boil crushed acorn meat, pour off brown water, add fresh water, repeat 5-7 times.
steps.helpDetail: Each boil removes tannins. The water turns dark brown — that's tannin leaching. Keep changing water until it stays clear.

steps.instruction: Taste test: a small piece should be mild and nutty with no bitterness. If bitter, continue leaching.
steps.helpDetail: Tannins are not toxic in survival quantities but cause nausea and impair digestion. Remove as much as practical.
steps.illustrationDescription: Taste test: a small piece shou

steps.instruction: Use leached acorn meal to make flatbread, porridge, or thickener for stews. Dry for long-term storage.
steps.helpDetail: Acorn flour has a pleasant nutty flavor. Mix with water, form patties, cook on a hot rock. High in fat and carbohydrates — excellent survival food.
steps.illustrationDescription: Use leached acorn meal to make

techniques.name: Bird Call Trap
techniques.subtitle: Attract birds for snaring using calls

steps.instruction: Learn to mimic distress calls — kissing the back of your hand rapidly creates a high-pitched squeaking.
steps.helpDetail: This squeaking sound mimics a small animal or bird in distress. Predatory and curious birds investigate these sounds.

steps.instruction: Set snares around a clearing or perch before calling. Use fine cordage in slip-noose loops.
steps.helpDetail: Set 4-6 small snares on branches and the ground around your calling position. Birds land to investigate — their feet catch in the loops.
steps.illustrationDescription: Set snares around a clearing o

steps.instruction: Hide in concealment 10-20 feet from the snare area. Make distress calls for 3-5 minutes, then wait 5 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Patience is critical. Birds may take 10+ minutes to respond. Alternate calling periods with silence. Movement spooks incoming birds.

steps.instruction: For waterfowl: create a blind near water and use splashing sounds to simulate feeding activity.
steps.helpDetail: Waterfowl respond to feeding sounds. Splashing water with a stick from behind a reed blind attracts nearby ducks and geese.

steps.instruction: Process caught birds by plucking (hot water dipping loosens feathers) and gutting. Cook thoroughly.
steps.helpDetail: All wild birds are edible when cooked. Some (like crows and gulls) taste poor but are nutritious. Roast over flame or boil in a stew.

techniques.name: Tree Sap Collection
techniques.subtitle: Tap trees for drinkable, calorie-rich sap

steps.instruction: Tappable trees: maple, birch, walnut, sycamore. Sap flows in late winter/early spring when nights are freezing and days thaw.
steps.helpDetail: The freeze-thaw cycle creates pressure that pushes sap upward. Sap flows when daytime temps are above 40°F and nights drop below 32°F.

steps.instruction: Drill or cut a 1-2 inch deep hole at a slight upward angle, 3 feet from the ground.
steps.helpDetail: Angle the hole upward so sap flows outward by gravity. Choose the south-facing side of the tree (warmest). 2-3 holes per large tree.
steps.illustrationDescription: Drill or cut a 1-2 inch deep h

steps.instruction: Insert a spile — a hollowed-out elderberry or sumac branch, or carved wooden spout.
steps.helpDetail: The spile channels sap from the hole into your collection container. Carve a 4-inch tube that fits snugly in the hole.

steps.instruction: Hang a container below the spout. A birch bark container, bottle, or pot works.
steps.helpDetail: Collection takes time — 1-3 liters per day per tap. Check and empty containers twice daily. Sap spoils quickly in warm weather.
steps.illustrationDescription: Hang a container below the spo

steps.instruction: Drink sap directly — it is mildly sweet and hydrating. Boil down for syrup (40:1 ratio for maple).
steps.helpDetail: Raw sap is mostly water with 2-3% sugar. It's a safe, clean water and calorie source. Boiling 40 liters of maple sap produces 1 liter of syrup.
steps.illustrationDescription: Drink sap directly — it is mil

techniques.name: Hand Fishing (Noodling)
techniques.subtitle: Catch fish by hand in shallow water

steps.instruction: Wade slowly through shallow water near undercut banks, submerged logs, and rock crevices.
steps.helpDetail: Fish shelter in holes and under structure. Move silently — vibrations travel far in water. Approach from downstream.

steps.instruction: Feel slowly under banks and into holes with your hands. Fish hiding in holes will not flee — they are cornered.
steps.helpDetail: Reach slowly. Fish often allow approaches if you don't make sudden movements. Feel for tail or body texture.
steps.illustrationDescription: Feel slowly under banks and in

steps.instruction: When you feel a fish, slide your hand forward to the gills. Grip firmly behind the gill plate.
steps.helpDetail: The gill grip is the most secure hold. Fingers hooked under the gill plate give you leverage. Squeeze firmly and lift.
steps.illustrationDescription: When you feel a fish, slide yo

steps.instruction: For flatfish (catfish, flounder): pin against the bottom with both hands, then scoop upward.
steps.helpDetail: Bottom-dwelling fish can be trapped against the stream bed. Use one hand as a backstop and scoop with the other.

steps.instruction: Caution: be aware of snakes, turtles, and crayfish in the same holes. Feel carefully before grabbing.
steps.helpDetail: Snapping turtles and water moccasins use the same habitat as fish. If you feel a shell or smooth round body, withdraw slowly.
steps.illustrationDescription: Caution: be aware of snakes, t

techniques.name: Rock Boiling
techniques.subtitle: Boil water and cook food without a fireproof container

steps.instruction: Create a watertight container: birch bark folded box, hollowed log, animal stomach, or depression in rock.
steps.helpDetail: The container does not need to be fireproof — it never touches the fire. Water keeps the container below its ignition point.

steps.instruction: Heat rocks in your fire for 30+ minutes until they glow or hiss when sprinkled with water.
steps.helpDetail: Use dense, dry rocks — NEVER river rocks (they can explode). Granite, basalt, and sandstone work well. Fist-sized or larger.
steps.illustrationDescription: Heat rocks in your fire for 30

steps.instruction: Use wooden tongs (split green sticks) to transfer hot rocks from fire to water container.
steps.helpDetail: Drop rocks gently — splashing scalding water is dangerous. Have 4-5 rocks heating at all times so you can rotate continuously.

steps.instruction: Each rock heats the water significantly. 3-4 rocks will bring a quart to a rolling boil.
steps.helpDetail: Replace rocks as they cool (they stop bubbling). A rotation of rocks maintains a continuous boil for cooking.
steps.illustrationDescription: Each rock heats the water sign

steps.instruction: Rock boiling can cook stews, soups, render fat, sterilize water, and process plant foods.
steps.helpDetail: This is THE fundamental cooking technique when you have no metal pot. It was the primary cooking method for thousands of years.
steps.illustrationDescription: Rock boiling can cook stews, s

techniques.name: Stinging Nettle Harvest
techniques.subtitle: Safely harvest and cook highly nutritious wild nettles

steps.instruction: Identify: tall plant with opposite, serrated, heart-shaped leaves. Covered in fine stinging hairs.
steps.helpDetail: Stinging nettle (Urtica dioica) grows worldwide in moist, nutrient-rich soil — near streams, forest edges, old homesteads.

steps.instruction: Harvest using gloves, thick cloth, or by gripping the stem firmly (the stingers fold flat under firm pressure).
steps.helpDetail: Pick the top 4-6 leaves — young growth is most tender and nutritious. Avoid flowering plants — they become gritty.

steps.instruction: Cooking neutralizes the sting completely. Boil for 2-3 minutes, steam, or sauté.
steps.helpDetail: Once cooked, nettles are safe to handle and eat. They taste like earthy spinach. The cooking water makes a mineral-rich broth.

steps.instruction: Nutritional powerhouse: more protein per weight than most vegetables, plus iron, calcium, and vitamins A/C.
steps.helpDetail: Nettles are arguably the most nutritious wild plant available. In survival, they provide essential minerals often missing from foraged diets.

steps.instruction: Dry leaves for tea or future cooking. Dried nettles store for months and retain nutrition.
steps.helpDetail: Dried nettle tea is a traditional health beverage. Leaf fibers can also be processed into strong cordage similar to linen.
steps.illustrationDescription: Dry leaves for tea or future c

techniques.sourceName: Peterson Field Guide – Edible Wild Plants

techniques.name: Crayfish Catching by Hand
techniques.subtitle: Catch freshwater crayfish without tools

steps.instruction: Search in shallow streams and ponds — lift rocks slowly and look for crayfish underneath.
steps.helpDetail: Crayfish shelter under rocks, logs, and debris during the day. They emerge at night to feed. Daytime hunting = turning over rocks.

steps.instruction: Approach from behind — crayfish escape by flipping their tail backward rapidly.
steps.helpDetail: Position your hand behind the crayfish before you grab. They always flee backward (tail-first). Cut off their escape route.

steps.instruction: Grasp firmly behind the claws, pinching the body between thumb and finger.
steps.helpDetail: The grip point is the carapace (body shell) behind the claw joints. Crayfish can pinch but are not dangerous — just startling.

steps.instruction: Collect in a container of stream water. Keep alive until ready to cook.
steps.helpDetail: Live crayfish stay fresh. Dead crayfish spoil rapidly and can cause illness. Cook within hours of catching.
steps.illustrationDescription: Collect in a container of stre

steps.instruction: Cook by boiling in water for 5-8 minutes until bright red. Eat the tail meat and claw meat.
steps.helpDetail: Break the tail off, peel the shell, and eat the white meat inside. Large claws also contain meat. Discard the head and body. 20-30 crayfish = a meal.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cook by boiling in water for 5

techniques.name: Solar Food Drying
techniques.subtitle: Preserve meat and plants using sun and wind

steps.instruction: Slice meat into thin strips (1/4 inch or thinner). Cut against the grain for easier eating.
steps.helpDetail: Thinner = faster drying. Cut with the grain for tougher jerky. Cut against the grain for more tender strips. Remove all fat — fat goes rancid.

steps.instruction: Build a drying rack: horizontal sticks between two uprights, 3-4 feet off the ground.
steps.helpDetail: Elevating keeps animals away and improves air circulation. Space sticks 1 inch apart. Face the rack into the prevailing wind.

steps.instruction: Drape strips over the rack sticks with space between each strip for airflow.
steps.helpDetail: Strips must not touch each other — touching spots stay moist and rot. Turn strips once during drying for even dehydration.
steps.illustrationDescription: Drape strips over the rack sti

steps.instruction: Build a small smoky fire beneath the rack to repel flies and add flavor.
steps.helpDetail: Flies lay eggs on drying meat within minutes. Smoke deters insects and adds preservative compounds. Use green wood for maximum smoke.
steps.illustrationDescription: Build a small smoky fire benea

steps.instruction: Meat is done when strips crack when bent but don't break completely. Store in a dry, sealed container.
steps.helpDetail: Properly dried jerky lasts weeks to months. If it bends like rubber, it needs more drying. If it snaps in half, it's over-dried but still edible.
steps.illustrationDescription: Meat is done when strips crack

techniques.name: Fishing Bait Collection
techniques.subtitle: Find natural bait for fishing

steps.instruction: Worms: drive a stake into moist ground and rub another stick across the top. Vibrations bring worms up.
steps.helpDetail: This 'worm grunting' technique mimics mole vibrations. Worms surface to escape perceived predators. Works best after rain.
steps.illustrationDescription: Worms: drive a stake into mois

steps.instruction: Grubs: peel bark from dead logs and rotting stumps. Beetle larvae (white grubs) live underneath.
steps.helpDetail: The fat, white C-shaped larvae of wood-boring beetles are found in every forest. They are excellent bait — and edible themselves.
steps.illustrationDescription: Grubs: peel bark from dead log

steps.instruction: Insects: catch grasshoppers in the early morning when they're sluggish from cold.
steps.helpDetail: Grasshoppers are top-tier bass bait. In early morning dew, they can't fly well and are easy to catch by hand. Store in a closed container.

steps.instruction: Crayfish: catch small ones (1-2 inches) for bait — tail meat is irresistible to bass and catfish.
steps.helpDetail: Peel the tail, thread the hook through the meat. Crayfish scent is a powerful attractant. The tail stays on the hook better than whole crayfish.

steps.instruction: Improvised bait: cheese, bread balls, corn, berries, raw meat scraps, even soap (contains fat that attracts catfish).
steps.helpDetail: If natural bait is unavailable, food scraps work. Mold bread into small balls around the hook. Fat-based baits work best for catfish.

techniques.name: Bone Grease Rendering
techniques.subtitle: Extract fat from bones for cooking and calories

steps.instruction: Crack long bones into 2-inch pieces using rocks. Smash thoroughly — expose the marrow and spongy interior.
steps.helpDetail: The more surface area exposed, the more fat renders out. Use a hard rock and flat anvil stone. Target long leg bones — they have the most fat.

steps.instruction: Place crushed bones in a pot or rock-boiling container with water. Bring to a sustained boil.
steps.helpDetail: The boiling water melts fat from the bone matrix. Use enough water to cover the bones completely. Keep at a rolling boil.
steps.illustrationDescription: Place crushed bones in a pot o

steps.instruction: Skim the fat that rises to the surface. Collect in a separate container.
steps.helpDetail: Bone grease floats. Use a bark spoon or cloth to skim. Multiple rounds of boiling and skimming extract maximum fat.
steps.illustrationDescription: Skim the fat that rises to the

steps.instruction: Let rendered fat cool and solidify. It stores for weeks in a cool, sealed container.
steps.helpDetail: Solid white fat = well-rendered. Any brown bits will go rancid faster — strain through cloth for purest storage.
steps.illustrationDescription: Let rendered fat cool and soli

steps.instruction: Use bone grease for cooking, pemmican making, waterproofing leather, and skin protection.
steps.helpDetail: Bone grease is the most calorie-dense food in a survival situation — 9 calories per gram. A critical resource for long-term survival.
steps.illustrationDescription: Use bone grease for cooking, p

techniques.name: Hot Stone Oven
techniques.subtitle: Build a stone oven for baking and cooking

steps.instruction: Dig a hole 2 feet deep, 2 feet wide. Line the bottom and sides with flat stones.
steps.helpDetail: The stones absorb and radiate heat evenly. Use dense, dry stones — NEVER river rocks (they can explode when heated).
steps.illustrationDescription: Dig a hole 2 feet deep, 2 feet

steps.instruction: Build a fire in the stone-lined pit and burn for 2+ hours until the stones are extremely hot.
steps.helpDetail: You want the stones to glow or be too hot to hold your hand near. This takes significant fuel. Plan ahead.
steps.illustrationDescription: Build a fire in the stone-line

steps.instruction: Rake out all coals and ash. Place food directly on the hot stones or on a bed of green leaves.
steps.helpDetail: Green leaves create a steam barrier that prevents burning and adds moisture. Wrap food in large, edible leaves for best results.
steps.illustrationDescription: Rake out all coals and ash. Pl

steps.instruction: Cover with more green leaves, then a layer of dirt or sand to seal in the heat.
steps.helpDetail: The sealed oven cooks like a slow cooker — radiating heat from all sides. This method cooks large pieces of meat or root vegetables evenly.

steps.instruction: Cook time: 1-2 hours for root vegetables, 3-4 hours for large meat pieces. Uncover carefully — steam is scalding.
steps.helpDetail: The earth oven is the most efficient cooking method in survival — it requires no pot, uses minimal fuel after initial heating, and cooks unattended.

techniques.name: Improvised Flour Sources
techniques.subtitle: Make flour from wild sources for bread and thickener

steps.instruction: Cattail root: harvest thick rhizomes, peel, and dry. Pound into starchy flour.
steps.helpDetail: Cattail rhizomes contain 30-40% starch. Available year-round. Dry sliced rhizomes in the sun, then grind between rocks.

steps.instruction: Acorns: shell, leach tannins by soaking/boiling, dry, and grind into a rich flour.
steps.helpDetail: White oak acorns have less tannin. Flour has a nutty, pleasant flavor. High in fat and carbs — excellent for flatbreads.

steps.instruction: Pine cambium: dry inner bark strips, then grind into fibrous flour.
steps.helpDetail: Pine flour is starchy and fibrous. Mix with other flour sources for better texture. All pine species work and are available year-round.
steps.illustrationDescription: Pine cambium: dry inner bark s

steps.instruction: Dried grass seeds: many grass species produce small seeds that can be collected and ground.
steps.helpDetail: Shake seed heads into a container. Winnow chaff by tossing in the wind. Grind seeds between stones into fine flour.

steps.instruction: Mix any flour with water to make dough. Cook as flatbread on hot stones, wrap around sticks for twist bread.
steps.helpDetail: Wild flour flatbreads are calorie-dense and filling. Add salt (from evaporated seawater or mineral deposits) for flavor.
steps.illustrationDescription: Mix any flour with water to ma

techniques.name: Ash Caking (Nixtamalization)
techniques.subtitle: Process grains with wood ash for better nutrition

steps.instruction: Mix clean hardwood ash with water to create an alkaline solution (lye water).
steps.helpDetail: The alkaline water breaks down corn/grain hulls and releases bound niacin (vitamin B3). Without this, a corn-only diet causes pellagra.
steps.illustrationDescription: Mix clean hardwood ash with wa

steps.instruction: Boil corn or grain kernels in the ash water for 1-2 hours until hulls begin to loosen.
steps.helpDetail: The kernels will swell and the outer hull will become soft and papery. Stir occasionally to prevent sticking.
steps.illustrationDescription: Boil corn or grain kernels in

steps.instruction: Drain and rinse thoroughly — wash at least 3 times to remove all ash residue.
steps.helpDetail: The lye taste is unpleasant if not rinsed. Rub kernels between your hands while rinsing to remove loosened hulls.

steps.instruction: The resulting product (hominy/nixtamal) can be eaten as-is, dried for storage, or ground into masa flour.
steps.helpDetail: Hominy is tender, flavorful, and significantly more nutritious than untreated grain. Ground masa is the basis of tortillas.

steps.instruction: This process increases calcium, iron, and niacin availability by 2-3x over unprocessed corn.
steps.helpDetail: Indigenous peoples discovered nixtamalization thousands of years ago. Societies that skipped this process suffered widespread niacin deficiency.

techniques.name: Primitive Grill Construction
techniques.subtitle: Build a grill grate from green wood

steps.instruction: Cut 4-6 green (living) sticks about 2 feet long, finger-thickness.
steps.helpDetail: Green wood resists burning long enough to cook food. Hardwoods (oak, maple) last longer than softwoods. Avoid resinous woods (pine, spruce).

steps.instruction: Drive two forked sticks into the ground on opposite sides of the fire pit, 18 inches tall.
steps.helpDetail: The forks support the crossbar. Drive them in firmly so they don't tip under the weight of food.
steps.illustrationDescription: Drive two forked sticks into t

steps.instruction: Lay a crossbar between the forks. Arrange remaining sticks perpendicular to the crossbar, creating a grate.
steps.helpDetail: Space the grate sticks close enough that food doesn't fall through. Lash to the crossbar with cordage or green bark strips.
steps.illustrationDescription: Lay a crossbar between the for

steps.instruction: Position the grate 6-8 inches above the coals — not the flames.
steps.helpDetail: Cooking over coals, not flames, gives even heat and doesn't burn the food. Let the fire burn down to a bed of hot coals before cooking.

steps.instruction: Grill meat, fish, roots, and mushrooms directly. Rotate food for even cooking. Replace charred grate sticks.
steps.helpDetail: Green sticks char slowly. When they become brittle, swap them out. A primitive grill cooks food faster and more evenly than a stick-spit.
steps.illustrationDescription: Grill meat, fish, roots, and m

techniques.name: Seaweed Foraging
techniques.subtitle: Identify and eat edible seaweed species

steps.instruction: RULE: all seaweed is technically edible, but some are tough or taste bad. Focus on soft, thin varieties.
steps.helpDetail: No seaweed is poisonous. Some are too tough to digest. Avoid blue-green algae (cyanobacteria) in fresh water — THAT is toxic.

steps.instruction: Sea lettuce (Ulva): thin, bright green sheets. Eat raw or cooked. Found worldwide in intertidal zones.
steps.helpDetail: Sea lettuce tastes mild and is high in protein, iron, and vitamins A and C. It's the easiest seaweed to identify and most palatable raw.

steps.instruction: Kelp: large brown fronds with a long stipe (stem). Cook or dry. The stipe can be pickled.
steps.helpDetail: Kelp is calorie-sparse but mineral-rich (iodine, potassium). Dried kelp provides critical iodine missing from most survival diets.

steps.instruction: Dulse (Palmaria): flat, reddish-purple fronds. Eat raw, dried, or fried. Tastes smoky/savory.
steps.helpDetail: Dulse has been eaten for thousands of years in coastal communities. It contains all trace minerals and is a complete protein source.

steps.instruction: Harvest from LIVING, attached plants — not dead seaweed washed on shore. Rinse in fresh water before eating.
steps.helpDetail: Beach-washed seaweed may harbor bacteria and parasites. Living, attached seaweed is clean. Rinse to remove salt and sand.

techniques.sourceName: Peterson Field Guide – Edible Wild Plants

techniques.name: Drag Snare Set
techniques.subtitle: Set a snare that allows prey to drag the snare, tiring itself

steps.instruction: Instead of anchoring the snare to an immovable object, attach it to a heavy drag — a log or branch.
steps.helpDetail: An anchored snare allows powerful animals to break free by pulling against the fixed point. A drag gives way, exhausting the animal.

steps.instruction: The drag should be heavy enough to slow the animal but not so heavy it can't move it at all.
steps.helpDetail: Match drag weight to target: rabbit = 5 lb branch, fox = 15 lb log, deer = 30+ lb log. Too heavy = animal breaks the snare.
steps.illustrationDescription: The drag should be heavy enoug

steps.instruction: The drag catches on brush and trees as the animal moves, preventing it from traveling far.
steps.helpDetail: The drag tangles in vegetation, progressively immobilizing the animal. It may only travel 50-100 feet before becoming thoroughly entangled.
steps.illustrationDescription: The drag catches on brush and

steps.instruction: Set the snare on a known game trail with the drag positioned behind the snare, uphill if possible.
steps.helpDetail: Uphill positioning means the animal drags weight uphill, tiring faster. The trail ensures animals pass through regularly.
steps.illustrationDescription: Set the snare on a known game

steps.instruction: Check drag snares every few hours. Follow the drag trail to find the entangled animal.
steps.helpDetail: A tangled drag leaves an obvious trail: disturbed brush, scraped ground, broken twigs. Dispatch the animal quickly and humanely.
steps.illustrationDescription: Check drag snares every few ho

techniques.name: Edible Insect Identification
techniques.subtitle: Know which insects are safe to eat

steps.instruction: SAFE: crickets, grasshoppers (remove legs — spiny), ants, termites, beetle grubs, earthworms.
steps.helpDetail: These insects are eaten worldwide and are high in protein (60-70% by dry weight) and fat. Available in virtually every environment.

steps.instruction: AVOID: brightly colored insects, hairy caterpillars, insects that smell strongly, stinging insects.
steps.helpDetail: Bright colors = warning (toxins). Hairy caterpillars cause irritation. Strong smell = chemical defense. Stingers contain venom.

steps.instruction: COOK all insects before eating when possible. Cooking kills parasites and improves flavor.
steps.helpDetail: Roast on a hot rock, skewer and hold over flames, or drop into boiling water. Cooked insects are crunchy and nutty — much more palatable than raw.

steps.instruction: Where to find: under rocks and logs (grubs, worms), in rotting wood (beetle larvae), in grass (grasshoppers, crickets).
steps.helpDetail: Turn rocks and logs slowly. Grubs hide in the soil interface underneath. Rotting wood harbors beetle larvae — break apart punky logs.
steps.illustrationDescription: Where to find: under rocks and

steps.instruction: Nutritional value: 100g of crickets = 21g protein, 6g fat, plus iron, calcium, B12.
steps.helpDetail: Insects are among the most protein-dense food sources available. In a survival situation, overcoming the disgust factor unlocks a vast, reliable food source.

techniques.name: Fish Smoking Technique
techniques.subtitle: Hot-smoke fish for preservation and flavor

steps.instruction: Gut and fillet fish. Make long, thin cuts to expose maximum surface area to smoke.
steps.helpDetail: Butterflied fish (split open but connected at the back) smoke evenly. Remove all guts, bloodline, and gills — they spoil fastest.
steps.illustrationDescription: Gut and fillet fish. Make long

steps.instruction: Brine in salt water for 30 minutes if salt is available. This firms the flesh and improves preservation.
steps.helpDetail: Salt draws moisture out and creates a hostile environment for bacteria. No salt? Soak in seawater for 20 minutes as an alternative.
steps.illustrationDescription: Brine in salt water for 30 min

steps.instruction: Build a smoking rack: green sticks arranged as a grate over a small fire pit, enclosed with a shelter frame.
steps.helpDetail: The enclosure traps smoke around the fish. Use a poncho, bark sheets, or green boughs to create a smoke chamber.
steps.illustrationDescription: Build a smoking rack: green st

steps.instruction: Use green wood for smoke: hickory, apple, alder, oak. Avoid resinous woods (pine, spruce) — they impart bitter resin.
steps.helpDetail: Green wood smolders and produces dense smoke. A mix of wood chips and larger pieces maintains consistent smoke production.
steps.illustrationDescription: Use green wood for smoke: hick

steps.instruction: Smoke for 4-8 hours at low heat (150-180°F). Fish is done when firm, golden-brown, and flakes with a fork.
steps.helpDetail: Hot-smoked fish lasts 3-7 days without refrigeration. Cold-smoked (higher rack, more smoke, less heat) lasts weeks but takes 24+ hours.
steps.illustrationDescription: Smoke for 4-8 hours at low hea

techniques.name: Salt Extraction
techniques.subtitle: Obtain salt from natural sources

steps.instruction: Coastal: boil seawater in a shallow container until all water evaporates. White crystals remain.
steps.helpDetail: A gallon of seawater yields about 4.5 oz of salt. Use a flat rock or bark tray for maximum surface area and faster evaporation.
steps.illustrationDescription: Coastal: boil seawater in a sh

steps.instruction: Inland: find salt licks — mineral deposits where animals congregate. Break off salt-crusted rock.
steps.helpDetail: Animals travel miles to visit salt licks. Look for well-worn game trails converging on bare mineral ground. Taste the soil — salty = salt deposit.

steps.instruction: Plant ash method: burn hardwood, dissolve ash in water, filter, and evaporate. The residue contains potassium salts.
steps.helpDetail: This produces potash, not sodium chloride, but it functions similarly as a seasoning and preservative. Hickory ash has the highest mineral content.

steps.instruction: Blood: animal blood contains significant salt. Use fresh blood as a soup base or seasoning.
steps.helpDetail: Blood contains all essential electrolytes including sodium, potassium, and iron. Cook blood into a broth — do not drink raw (parasite risk).

steps.instruction: Salt is essential for electrolyte balance, food preservation, and hide tanning. Prioritize finding a source.
steps.helpDetail: Without salt intake, the body loses function within days under exertion and heat. Salt loss causes muscle cramps, confusion, and eventually shock.

techniques.name: Deadfall Trap Variations
techniques.subtitle: Build multiple deadfall trap designs for different prey

steps.instruction: Figure-4 deadfall: three sticks notched to interlock, supporting a heavy stone. Bait stick triggers collapse.
