steps.helpDetail: The figure-4 is the most versatile deadfall. Size the rock to the target animal: 5x body weight minimum for a clean kill.

steps.instruction: Paiute deadfall: uses a toggle and string trigger for more sensitivity than the figure-4.
steps.helpDetail: The Paiute triggers with less force, making it better for mice and small rodents. The string-toggle mechanism provides a hair trigger.

steps.instruction: Split-stick deadfall: a vertical stick holds the rock up. Bait is placed inside a split in the upright.
steps.helpDetail: When the animal pulls the bait, the split stick closes, releasing the support, and the rock falls. Simple and effective.

steps.instruction: Place multiple deadfalls along game trails, near burrow openings, and at identified feeding areas.
steps.helpDetail: More traps = higher catch probability. Set 10-20 deadfalls across an area. Check twice daily — trapped animals attract predators.

steps.instruction: Test the trigger before setting the weight stone. The trigger should release with a light touch.
steps.helpDetail: A trap that doesn't trigger is wasted effort. Test by gently touching the bait stick — the trigger should fire immediately.

techniques.name: Wild Rice Harvest
techniques.subtitle: Identify and harvest wild rice from waterways

steps.instruction: Wild rice (Zizania) grows in shallow lakes, ponds, and slow streams. Tall grass 4-8 feet with grain heads in late summer.
steps.helpDetail: Found across northern temperate regions. The grain heads droop and darken when ripe (late August-September in northern hemisphere).

steps.instruction: Harvest from a canoe/raft: bend the stalks over the vessel and knock the ripe seeds into the bottom.
steps.helpDetail: Use a stick to gently tap the grain heads. Ripe seeds fall easily. Unripe seeds stay attached — you can return for them later.

steps.instruction: Dry the harvested grain in the sun for 1-2 days. Stir occasionally to prevent mold.
steps.helpDetail: Spread seed thinly on a bark tray or rock surface. Fully dried grains rattle when shaken — that's your indicator of dryness.
steps.illustrationDescription: Dry the harvested grain in the

steps.instruction: Parch (lightly roast) the dried grain over low heat to loosen the hulls. Stir constantly.
steps.helpDetail: Parching makes the hull brittle and separable. Use a bark tray near coals, not over flames. The grain should darken slightly, not burn.

steps.instruction: Hull by rubbing between hands or treading in a bark container. Winnow by tossing in the wind.
steps.helpDetail: Chaff blows away, heavy grain falls back into the container. Wild rice cooks the same as cultivated rice — boil for 30-45 minutes.

techniques.sourceName: Peterson Field Guide – Edible Wild Plants

techniques.name: Fat Rendering Techniques
techniques.subtitle: Render clean cooking fat from animal carcasses

steps.instruction: Collect all fat from the carcass: kidney fat (suet), belly fat, and fat trimmed from meat.
steps.helpDetail: Kidney fat produces the cleanest, hardest tallow. Belly fat renders well but is softer. Save every scrap — fat is the most precious survival calorie.
steps.illustrationDescription: Collect all fat from the carca

steps.instruction: Cut fat into small pieces (1/2 inch cubes) or grind for faster rendering.
steps.helpDetail: Smaller pieces = more surface area = faster and more complete rendering. Remove any meat attached — meat scraps cause rancidity.

steps.instruction: Heat slowly in a pot or on a rock near the fire. Do NOT let it boil or smoke.
steps.helpDetail: Low, steady heat melts the fat without burning. If it smokes, remove from heat — burnt fat tastes terrible and degrades faster.
steps.illustrationDescription: Heat slowly in a pot or on a r

steps.instruction: Strain the liquid fat through cloth into a clean container. Discard the solid cracklings (or eat them).
steps.helpDetail: Cracklings are the defatted tissue remnants. They're edible, crunchy, and calorie-dense. The strained liquid fat is pure tallow/lard.

steps.instruction: Clean rendered fat solidifies into white, odorless tallow that stores for months.
steps.helpDetail: Tallow lasts 6+ months without refrigeration when kept cool and sealed. It's the basis for pemmican, soap, candles, and waterproofing.

techniques.name: Natural Refrigeration Methods
techniques.subtitle: Keep food cold without electricity

steps.instruction: Cold stream method: seal food in waterproof containers and submerge in a cold stream.
steps.helpDetail: Running water maintains a consistent 40-50°F temperature year-round. Anchor containers with rocks so they don't float away.

steps.instruction: Evaporative cooling: wrap food in wet cloth and place in the wind or shade.
steps.helpDetail: Evaporation absorbs heat. A wet burlap sack in the wind drops internal temperature 10-20°F below ambient. Re-wet as it dries.

steps.instruction: Underground cache: dig a hole 2-3 feet deep in shade. Line with rocks. Place food inside and cover.
steps.helpDetail: Ground temperature 2 feet deep is typically 50-55°F year-round, regardless of surface temperature. A natural root cellar.

steps.instruction: Snow/ice cache: bury food in snow or pack in natural ice. Snow insulates at 32°F.
steps.helpDetail: A snow-buried cache keeps food frozen through summer in some mountain environments. Dig below the snow surface for compacted, insulating layers.
steps.illustrationDescription: Snow/ice cache: bury food in s

steps.instruction: Hang food from a tree in shade: keeps it away from ground predators and in the cooler air above.
steps.helpDetail: Bear bag technique: hang 12+ feet high, 6+ feet from the trunk, 4+ feet below the branch. This protects from bears AND keeps food cooler.
steps.illustrationDescription: Hang food from a tree in shade

techniques.name: Pit Trap
techniques.subtitle: Build a concealed pit trap for medium game

steps.instruction: Dig a pit on an active game trail — 3 feet deep, 2 feet wide for rabbit-sized prey. Deeper for larger animals.
steps.helpDetail: The pit should be narrow enough that the animal can't turn around inside. Vertical walls prevent climbing out.
steps.illustrationDescription: Dig a pit on an active game tr

steps.instruction: Drive sharpened stakes into the bottom of the pit, pointing upward.
steps.helpDetail: Stakes ensure a quick, humane kill. Without stakes, trapped animals may escape by jumping. Harden stake tips by fire-hardening.

steps.instruction: Cover the pit with thin sticks, leaves, and debris to match the surrounding ground.
steps.helpDetail: The covering must support the weight of the surface materials but collapse under the animal's weight. Test carefully.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cover the pit with thin sticks

steps.instruction: Funnel the game trail into the pit using brush barriers on either side.
steps.helpDetail: Barriers reduce the trail width so animals must cross directly over the pit. Natural-looking barriers are less suspicious.
steps.illustrationDescription: Funnel the game trail into the

steps.instruction: Check pits every few hours. Mark pit locations clearly so YOU don't fall in.
steps.helpDetail: An unmarked pit trap near camp is dangerous to humans. Always approach from the marked side. Cover unused pits when leaving the area.
steps.illustrationDescription: Check pits every few hours. Ma

techniques.name: Rabbit Processing
techniques.subtitle: Skin, gut, and prepare a rabbit for cooking

steps.instruction: Pinch the skin at the middle of the back and make a small cut through the skin only — not the muscle.
steps.helpDetail: Rabbit skin is thin and separates easily from the muscle. The back pinch gives you a safe starting point away from organs.
steps.illustrationDescription: Pinch the skin at the middle o

steps.instruction: Insert two fingers into the cut and pull in opposite directions — the skin peels off like a shirt.
steps.helpDetail: Rabbit skin tears cleanly. Pull toward the head and toward the tail simultaneously. The feet and head require a knife to cut through.
steps.illustrationDescription: Insert two fingers into the cu

steps.instruction: Cut off the head and feet with a knife. Make a small cut at the base of the belly to open the cavity.
steps.helpDetail: Start the belly cut at the base, not the top, to avoid puncturing the bladder or intestines. Cut carefully — shallow cuts only.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cut off the head and feet with

steps.instruction: Reach in and pull all organs out in one mass. Save the heart, liver, and kidneys — discard the rest.
steps.helpDetail: The organs separate from the body cavity easily. The liver should be smooth and dark red. If it's spotted or pale, there may be disease — discard.
steps.illustrationDescription: Reach in and pull all organs o

steps.instruction: Rinse the cavity with clean water. Cook immediately — rabbit has almost no fat and spoils quickly.
steps.helpDetail: CRITICAL: rabbit starvation is real. Rabbit meat is too lean (2-3% fat) to sustain life alone. You MUST supplement with fat sources or you'll starve with a full stomach.
steps.illustrationDescription: Rinse the cavity with clean wa

techniques.name: Cordage Snare Sets
techniques.subtitle: Set 10+ snare traps for maximum catch probability

steps.instruction: Make 10-20 snare loops from cordage, wire, fishing line, or inner paracord strands.
steps.helpDetail: More snares = higher catch probability. One snare has maybe a 10% daily catch rate. Ten snares = 65% chance of catching SOMETHING.

steps.instruction: Size the loop to the target: rabbit = 4-inch diameter, squirrel = 2-inch, deer = 10-inch.
steps.helpDetail: The loop should be about fist-sized for rabbits. Hang so the bottom of the loop is 2-3 inches above the ground on the trail.
steps.illustrationDescription: Size the loop to the target: r

steps.instruction: Set snares on active game trails — look for fresh droppings, tracks, disturbed vegetation.
steps.helpDetail: Active signs: fresh scat, prints in mud, chewed vegetation, hair caught on thorns/branches. Stale trails have old, dried sign.
steps.illustrationDescription: Set snares on active game trai

steps.instruction: Use guide sticks to funnel animals through the snare — narrow the trail with sticks on either side.
steps.helpDetail: Animals follow the path of least resistance. Guide sticks force them through the snare loop rather than around it.
steps.illustrationDescription: Use guide sticks to funnel ani

steps.instruction: Anchor snares to a strong stake or tree. For larger animals, use a spring pole to lift and strangle quickly.
steps.helpDetail: A spring pole (bent sapling) jerks the animal off the ground when triggered, preventing chewing through the snare and making a humane kill.
steps.illustrationDescription: Anchor snares to a strong stak

techniques.name: Wild Root Vegetables
techniques.subtitle: Identify and harvest edible wild root vegetables

steps.instruction: Jerusalem artichoke (Helianthus tuberosus): follows sunflower-like tall plant. Knobby tubers at the root.
steps.helpDetail: Found across North America in disturbed soil, roadsides, and field edges. Tubers are crisp and sweet — edible raw or cooked.

steps.instruction: Wild carrot (Queen Anne's lace): feathery leaves, white flower cluster, hairy stem. Dig the first-year white root.
steps.helpDetail: CAUTION: poison hemlock looks similar but has smooth, purple-spotted stems and NO hair. Always verify hairy stem before eating.

steps.instruction: Burdock root: large, ruffled leaves, burr-producing plant. Dig the first-year taproot.
steps.helpDetail: Burdock root tastes like a mild artichoke heart. Peel, slice, and boil or roast. The root can be 2+ feet deep — dig carefully.

steps.instruction: Wapato (Sagittaria): arrow-shaped leaves in shallow water. Tubers at the root in mud.
steps.helpDetail: Wade into shallow water and feel with your feet. The tubers break free from mud when disturbed. They float — collect downstream.

steps.instruction: All wild root vegetables should be cooked to improve digestibility and destroy potential irritants.
steps.helpDetail: Boiling, roasting, or pit-cooking breaks down starches and inactivates compounds that cause digestive issues in raw roots.

techniques.sourceName: Peterson Field Guide – Edible Wild Plants

techniques.name: Animal Track Identification
techniques.subtitle: Read tracks to determine prey species and direction

steps.instruction: Deer: split heart-shaped hooves, 2-3 inches long. Walking stride 14-18 inches.
steps.helpDetail: Deer tracks are the most common large game sign. Fresh tracks show sharp edges in mud. Old tracks have softened, crumbled edges.

steps.instruction: Rabbit: hind feet (long, 3-4 in) land AHEAD of front feet (small, round, 1 in) when hopping.
steps.helpDetail: The distinctive Y-pattern with large hind prints in front is unmistakable. Rabbits are abundant and a reliable food source.

steps.instruction: Turkey: 3-toed, large (4 inches). Scratchings in leaf litter indicate feeding areas.
steps.helpDetail: Turkeys feed in the same areas repeatedly. Scratched-up patches of forest floor surrounded by turkey tracks = set snares here.

steps.instruction: Raccoon: hand-like prints with 5 distinct toes. Often found near water sources.
steps.helpDetail: Raccoon tracks near water indicate a reliable food-procurement area. Raccoons are nocturnal — set traps at their waterside feeding spots.

steps.instruction: Track freshness: sharp edges = hours old. Softened edges = days old. Water-filled = recent (after last rain).
steps.helpDetail: Only invest trapping effort on trails with FRESH sign. Days-old tracks mean the animal may have moved to a different area.

techniques.name: Paiute Deadfall Trap
techniques.subtitle: A highly sensitive, lightning-fast trigger for small game

steps.instruction: The Paiute deadfall is faster and more sensitive than a Figure-4, making it better for fast mice and rats.
steps.helpDetail: It requires a piece of cordage, a heavy flat rock, a Y-shaped vertical stick, a straight lever stick, a bait stick, and a tiny toggle stick.

steps.instruction: Drive the Y-shaped vertical stick firmly into the ground.
steps.helpDetail: Balance the straight lever stick in the crotch of the Y. One end holds up the heavy rock, the other end extends outward.

steps.instruction: Tie the cordage to the outward end of the lever stick.
steps.helpDetail: Tie the tiny toggle stick to the other end of the cordage.
steps.illustrationDescription: Tie the cordage to the outward

steps.instruction: Wrap the cordage halfway around the vertical Y-stick.
steps.helpDetail: Catch the tiny toggle against the vertical stick, using the long bait stick to pin it in place horizontally.

steps.instruction: Bait the end of the bait stick under the rock.
steps.helpDetail: When the animal nudges the bait stick, it slips, releasing the toggle, which releases the cord, which drops the lever and the rock instantly.
steps.illustrationDescription: Bait the end of the bait stick

techniques.name: Arapuca Bird Trap
techniques.subtitle: A pyramid cage trap for capturing birds alive

steps.instruction: Construct a pyramid-shaped cage out of small, straight twigs.
steps.helpDetail: Start with a square base of four sticks. Stack smaller sticks on top, log-cabin style, angling slightly inward until they form a closed peak. Tie them securely.

steps.instruction: Carve a trigger system: a long stake, a toggle stick, and a catch stick.
steps.helpDetail: Drive the long stake into the ground inside the drop zone.
steps.illustrationDescription: Carve a trigger system: a long

steps.instruction: Prop up one edge of the pyramid cage using the catch stick.
steps.helpDetail: Tie a cord with the toggle to the center peak of the cage. Loop it under the stake in the ground, and pin the toggle against the stake using a baited trigger stick.
steps.illustrationDescription: Prop up one edge of the pyrami

steps.instruction: The pyramid acts as a heavy, weighted cage.
steps.helpDetail: When a bird lands and pecks the baited trigger stick, the toggle flies off, and the heavy pyramid drops over the bird, trapping it alive.
steps.illustrationDescription: The pyramid acts as a heavy, w

steps.instruction: Check the trap frequently.
steps.helpDetail: Living birds will panic and eventually find a way to escape or be eaten by predators if left alone.

techniques.name: Spring Pole Snare
techniques.subtitle: Hoist an animal into the air to prevent predators from stealing it

steps.instruction: Find a flexible, strong sapling near an animal trail to act as your spring engine.
steps.helpDetail: Trim the branches off the sapling so it doesn't get tangled when it springs upward.

steps.instruction: Bend the sapling down to the trail.
steps.helpDetail: Select a spot where the tension is strong enough to lift the target animal (e.g., a 5 lb rabbit requires a sapling with significant pull).
steps.illustrationDescription: Bend the sapling down to the t

steps.instruction: Construct a trigger mechanism (like a toggle) and drive an anchor stake into the ground.
steps.helpDetail: Tie your snare wire/cord to the sapling, running it through the trigger toggle.

steps.instruction: Set the trigger and arrange the snare loop vertically over the trail.
steps.helpDetail: Use small twigs to prop the loop open. The loop should be about 4 inches in diameter for a rabbit, sitting 2 inches off the ground.
steps.illustrationDescription: Set the trigger and arrange th

steps.instruction: When triggered, the sapling violently snaps upright.
steps.helpDetail: The snare tightens instantly and yanks the animal several feet into the air, killing it instantly and keeping it out of reach of foxes or coyotes.

techniques.name: Squirrel Snare Pole
techniques.subtitle: Catch multiple squirrels descending a tree

steps.instruction: Find a long, thick logging pole or branch (4-6 inches diameter, 6-10 feet long).
steps.helpDetail: Squirrels prefer an easy bridge rather than jumping or climbing rough bark. Lean this pole against a tree where you have seen squirrel activity.
steps.illustrationDescription: Find a long, thick logging pol

steps.instruction: Cut 4 to 6 wire snares. The loops should be 2.5 inches in diameter.
steps.helpDetail: Use brass or steel snare wire. Paracord will be chewed through instantly.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cut 4 to 6 wire snares. The lo

steps.instruction: Attach the snares sequentially up the length of the pole.
steps.helpDetail: Space them about 18 inches apart. Ensure they are tied securely to the pole so the squirrel can't drag it away.

steps.instruction: Position each loop so it stands upright on the top face of the pole.
steps.helpDetail: A squirrel running down the pole will run headfirst right into the loops rather than taking the time to climb around them.
steps.illustrationDescription: Position each loop so it stand

steps.instruction: The squirrel gets caught and falls off the side of the pole, hanging.
steps.helpDetail: Because the pole has multiple snares, other squirrels will simply jump over the hanging one and get caught in the next snare down.
steps.illustrationDescription: The squirrel gets caught and f

techniques.name: River Fish Weir
techniques.subtitle: Construct a funnel trap to catch fish in moving water

steps.instruction: Locate a shallow, narrow section of a stream or tidal inlet.
steps.helpDetail: The water should be knee-deep or less, and the flow must be steady but not violent enough to wash away your trap.

steps.instruction: Drive heavy wooden stakes into the riverbed in a 'V' shape.
steps.helpDetail: The wide opening of the 'V' should face upstream to catch fish swimming along the current, or face downstream if catching migratory fish swimming up.

steps.instruction: Weave smaller branches, reeds, or rocks between the stakes.
steps.helpDetail: Create a solid fence that lets water through but blocks fish. The walls of the 'V' funnel the fish toward the point.

steps.instruction: At the point of the 'V', leave a small opening and build a circular holding pen.
steps.helpDetail: Once the fish swim through the narrow opening into the pen, they will become confused and unable to find the small exit against the current.
steps.illustrationDescription: At the point of the 'V', leave

steps.instruction: Wade in with a spear or net to harvest the trapped fish.
steps.helpDetail: Check the weir daily and repair breaches caused by debris in the river.
steps.illustrationDescription: Wade in with a spear or net to

techniques.name: Pine Inner Bark (Cambium)
techniques.subtitle: Harvesting survival calories from trees

steps.instruction: Identify a healthy Pine, Birch, or Spruce tree.
steps.helpDetail: Avoid taking bark from an entire ring around the tree (girdling), as this will kill the tree. Take vertical strips.

steps.instruction: Chop away the rough, dark outer bark using a knife or axe.
steps.helpDetail: The outer bark is dead, highly tannic, and entirely indigestible.
steps.illustrationDescription: Chop away the rough, dark oute

steps.instruction: Scrape off the soft, wet, white layer (the cambium) underneath.
steps.helpDetail: This thin layer lies directly against the hard wood of the tree trunk. It is rich in starches, sugars, and vitamin C.
steps.illustrationDescription: Scrape off the soft, wet, whit

steps.instruction: Eat it raw, boil it into a broth, or dry it and grind it into flour.
steps.helpDetail: Frying it in animal fat makes it highly palatable, resembling potato chips. Boiling removes the turpentine taste common in pine.
steps.illustrationDescription: Eat it raw, boil it into a bro

steps.instruction: Pine needles can also be chopped and steeped in hot (not boiling) water for a massive Vitamin C boost.
steps.helpDetail: Boiling destroys the vitamin C. Steep like normal tea.
steps.illustrationDescription: Pine needles can also be chopp

techniques.name: Broadleaf Plantain (Plantago)
techniques.subtitle: A ubiquitous edible and medicinal weed

steps.instruction: Identify Broadleaf Plantain (Plantago major) in lawns, trails, and disturbed dirt.
steps.helpDetail: It features broad, oval, ribbed leaves that grow in a low rosette formation close to the soil.

steps.instruction: Check the distinct rubbery veins.
steps.helpDetail: If you snap a leaf stem and pull it apart, strong, elastic, stringy veins will emerge — the definitive identifier.

steps.instruction: Harvest the smallest, youngest leaves from the center.
steps.helpDetail: Older, large leaves are extremely tough and bitter. Young leaves can be eaten raw in a salad.

steps.instruction: Boil the older leaves.
steps.helpDetail: Boiling destroys the tough fibers, making them edible as a cooked green, similar to spinach.

steps.instruction: Use crushed leaves medicinally.
steps.helpDetail: Chew a leaf to make a 'poultice' and press it onto bee stings, mosquito bites, or minor cuts. The drawing action relieves pain and swelling instantly.

techniques.name: Cattail Root Foraging
techniques.subtitle: Harvesting 'the supermarket of the swamp'

steps.instruction: Locate cattails in shallow water or muddy shorelines.
steps.helpDetail: Ensure the water is not polluted by industrial runoff, as cattails absorb heavy metals and toxins.

steps.instruction: Wade in and dig deep into the mud around the base of the stalk.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT pull by the stalk, it will snap. You must dig beneath the thick, horizontal rootstock (rhizome).
steps.illustrationDescription: Wade in and dig deep into the

steps.instruction: Pull up the thick, ropey rhizome.
steps.helpDetail: Wash the thick black mud off. The rhizome consists of a spongy outer layer protecting a core of pure starch.
steps.illustrationDescription: Pull up the thick, ropey rhizo

steps.instruction: Peel the outer rind.
steps.helpDetail: Use a knife to strip the spongy rind, revealing the white core. It can be eaten raw, but boiling or roasting is much safer to kill waterborne parasites.

steps.instruction: Extract the starch.
steps.helpDetail: Pound the roots in cold water, let the starch settle to the bottom, pour off the water, and let the wet starch dry into a brilliant white flour.

techniques.name: Purslane Foraging
techniques.subtitle: Identify and harvest a highly nutritious succulent weed

steps.instruction: Identify Purslane (Portulaca oleracea) growing flat on the ground in sunny, dry areas, sidewalks, or gardens.
steps.helpDetail: It has a distinct red, smooth, thick stem that creeps radially outward across the ground.

steps.instruction: Examine the leaves: They are fleshy, smooth, paddle-shaped, and succulent (thick).
steps.helpDetail: Break the stem. If it produces a clear, watery juice, it is Purslane. If it bleeds a milky white sap, it is Spurge (POISONOUS!).
steps.illustrationDescription: Examine the leaves: They are f

steps.instruction: Purslane is a survival superfood, containing more Omega-3 fatty acids than any other leafy green plant.
steps.helpDetail: It also provides massive amounts of Vitamin A, C, and magnesium.

steps.instruction: Harvest the entire plant by pulling it up.
steps.helpDetail: Rinse the grit out. Everything is edible: the stems, leaves, flowers, and tiny black seeds.
steps.illustrationDescription: Harvest the entire plant by pu

steps.instruction: Eat it raw or boiled.
steps.helpDetail: Raw, it has a crisp texture and a slightly sour, lemony flavor. Boiling it will make it slightly mucilaginous (slimy, like okra), which acts perfectly as a soup thickener.

techniques.name: Smoke Preservation of Meat
techniques.subtitle: Drying and smoking wild game for long-term storage

steps.instruction: Cut the meat into thin strips, no thicker than 1/4 inch.
steps.helpDetail: Thicker strips will rot in the center before the smoke penetrates.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cut the meat into thin strips,

steps.instruction: Build a small, low-heat smoldering fire.
steps.helpDetail: Use green wood or wet wood to produce maximum smoke with minimal flame. Hickory, oak, and mesquite produce the best results.

steps.instruction: Construct a drying rack 2–3 feet above the smoke source.
steps.helpDetail: Drape the thin meat strips over the rack so smoke flows across all surfaces.

steps.instruction: Maintain the smoke continuously for 12–24 hours.
steps.helpDetail: The smoke contains formaldehyde, acetic acid, and creosote, which kill surface bacteria and seal the meat.

steps.instruction: Properly smoked jerky can last weeks at ambient temperature.
steps.helpDetail: Store in a cool, dry, breathable container. If it develops a slimy texture or sour smell, it has spoiled and must be discarded.

techniques.name: Grub and Larva Consumption
techniques.subtitle: Finding and eating high-calorie insect larvae

steps.instruction: Look under rotting logs, peeling bark, and in the soil around dead trees.
steps.helpDetail: Beetle larvae (grubs) are fat, white, C-shaped, and extremely high in fat and protein.

steps.instruction: Palm weevil larvae, witchetty grubs, and mealworms are all safe and highly nutritious.
steps.helpDetail: Avoid brightly colored larvae (orange, red, yellow) which may be toxic.

steps.instruction: Pinch the head off, as mandibles can bite your throat on the way down.
steps.helpDetail: Squeeze gently to expel gut contents (which may contain rotten wood or dirt).
steps.illustrationDescription: Pinch the head off, as mandibl

steps.instruction: Eat raw or roasted on a hot rock near the fire.
steps.helpDetail: Roasting dramatically improves the taste (they become crispy and nutty) and kills any parasites.
steps.illustrationDescription: Eat raw or roasted on a hot ro

steps.instruction: A handful of large grubs provides more calories than a handful of berries.
steps.helpDetail: Insects are the most abundant and reliable calorie source on the planet.
steps.illustrationDescription: A handful of large grubs provi

techniques.name: Crayfish Catching
techniques.subtitle: Hand-catching freshwater lobsters from stream beds

steps.instruction: Look in shallow, rocky streams with clear water.
steps.helpDetail: Crayfish hide under rocks, submerged logs, and overhanging banks during the day.

steps.instruction: Slowly lift rocks and look underneath.
steps.helpDetail: Crayfish will dart backwards when startled. Position your hand behind them before lifting the rock.
steps.illustrationDescription: Slowly lift rocks and look und

steps.instruction: Grab from behind, pinching the back of the carapace (body shell).
steps.helpDetail: Their powerful claws face forward. Approaching from behind avoids a painful pinch.

steps.instruction: Cook by boiling for 5 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: NEVER eat crayfish raw. Freshwater crustaceans carry lung flukes (Paragonimus) which cause serious parasitic infection.

steps.instruction: Eat the tail meat.
steps.helpDetail: Twist off the tail, peel the shell, and pull out the white meat. The claws of large crayfish also contain edible meat.

techniques.name: Complete Dandelion Use
techniques.subtitle: Every part of the dandelion is edible — leaves, roots, flowers

steps.instruction: Identify dandelions by their jagged, tooth-shaped leaves ('dent de lion') growing in a rosette from a central taproot.
steps.helpDetail: Avoid lookalikes like cat's ear — check for a hollow, milky stem (dandelion) vs. solid, hairy stem (cat's ear).

steps.instruction: Harvest young leaves before the plant flowers.
steps.helpDetail: Young leaves are tender and mildly bitter. Older leaves after flowering are extremely bitter.

steps.instruction: Eat leaves raw in a salad or boil to reduce bitterness.
steps.helpDetail: Dandelion greens are extremely rich in Vitamins A, C, and K, plus iron and calcium.
steps.illustrationDescription: Eat leaves raw in a salad or b

steps.instruction: Roast the taproot, grind it, and steep in hot water.
steps.helpDetail: This produces a passable coffee substitute, used extensively during wartime rationing.
steps.illustrationDescription: Roast the taproot, grind it, a

steps.instruction: Flower heads can be eaten raw, battered and fried, or steeped into tea.
steps.helpDetail: The entire plant is medicinal: a mild diuretic, liver tonic, and anti-inflammatory.
steps.illustrationDescription: Flower heads can be eaten raw,

techniques.name: Palm Heart Harvesting
techniques.subtitle: Extracting the edible growing tip from palm trees

steps.instruction: The heart of palm is the soft, white growing tip found inside the top of the trunk.
steps.helpDetail: It tastes like artichoke hearts and is extremely nutritious.
steps.illustrationDescription: The heart of palm is the soft,

steps.instruction: Fell a young palm tree (easier than a mature one).
steps.helpDetail: Cut through the trunk with a machete or improvised axe.

steps.instruction: Strip the outer layers of trunk until you reach the soft, white inner core.
steps.helpDetail: The edible portion is 6-12 inches long and 2-3 inches diameter.
steps.illustrationDescription: Strip the outer layers of trun

steps.instruction: Eat raw or sliced into soups.
steps.helpDetail: Do not eat the fibrous outer layers; they are inedible.
steps.illustrationDescription: Eat raw or sliced into soups.

steps.instruction: WARNING: Harvesting kills the tree.
steps.helpDetail: Only harvest from abundant palm groves. Some species (like coconut) are too valuable alive for their ongoing fruit production.

techniques.name: Coconut Water Safety
techniques.subtitle: Using coconuts correctly for hydration and calories

steps.instruction: Green (young) coconuts contain the most water and have thin, soft meat.
steps.helpDetail: This water is safe to drink and is isotonic (similar to blood plasma).

steps.instruction: Brown (mature) coconuts have less water but thick, calorie-dense meat.
steps.helpDetail: The water in brown coconuts can cause diarrhea if consumed in large quantities.

steps.instruction: Limit coconut water intake to 3-4 coconuts per day.
steps.helpDetail: Excessive consumption causes loose bowels due to high magnesium content, accelerating dehydration.

steps.instruction: Coconut meat provides fat, protein, and calories.
steps.helpDetail: Scrape it out with a sharp shell fragment and eat fresh.

steps.instruction: Use the husk fiber for cordage and the shell as a container or bowl.
steps.helpDetail: Every part of the coconut has survival utility.
steps.illustrationDescription: Use the husk fiber for cordage

techniques.name: Reflector Oven Baking
techniques.subtitle: Building an oven from reflective material for campfire baking

steps.instruction: Construct a V-shaped reflector from aluminum foil, flat bark, or sheet metal.
steps.helpDetail: The opening faces the fire at a distance of 12-18 inches.

steps.instruction: Place a flat cooking surface (rock, metal sheet) inside the V.
steps.helpDetail: Food sits on this surface and is cooked by radiated heat from the fire bouncing off the reflectors.

steps.instruction: The reflector focuses the fire's radiant heat onto the food from multiple angles.
steps.helpDetail: This creates oven-like conditions sufficient to bake bread, biscuits, and roast meat.

steps.instruction: Adjust distance from fire to control temperature.
steps.helpDetail: Closer = hotter. The sweet spot is where you can hold your hand at food level for exactly 3 seconds (about 375°F).
steps.illustrationDescription: Adjust distance from fire to c

steps.instruction: Rotate the food periodically for even cooking.
steps.helpDetail: The side facing the fire browns faster. Turn every 10 minutes.

techniques.name: Underground Rock Oven
techniques.subtitle: Pit-cooking large meals using fire-heated stones

steps.instruction: Dig a pit 2 feet deep and 2 feet wide.
steps.helpDetail: Line it with rocks. Avoid river rocks — they can contain trapped moisture and EXPLODE violently when heated.
steps.illustrationDescription: Dig a pit 2 feet deep and 2 fe

steps.instruction: Build a large fire in the pit and burn it for 2 hours.
steps.helpDetail: The rocks must be superheated — glowing red if possible.
steps.illustrationDescription: Build a large fire in the pit

steps.instruction: Rake out the coals and ash.
steps.helpDetail: Leave the hot rocks in place at the bottom.
steps.illustrationDescription: Rake out the coals and ash.

steps.instruction: Layer food (wrapped in non-toxic leaves like banana, burdock, or corn husks) on the hot rocks.
steps.helpDetail: Cover with more leaves, then wet burlap or a tarp, then 6 inches of soil.

steps.instruction: Cook for 2-4 hours.
steps.helpDetail: The food steam-bakes inside the sealed pit. This method can cook entire animals, root vegetables, and bread simultaneously.

techniques.name: Tripod Cooking Setup
techniques.subtitle: Building an adjustable fire-suspended cooking system

steps.instruction: Lash three sturdy poles together at the top using a tripod lashing.
steps.helpDetail: The poles should be 5-6 feet long and thick enough to support a full pot of water.

steps.instruction: Spread the legs and set the tripod over your fire.
steps.helpDetail: The pot hangs from a chain, wire, or cord attached to the apex.
steps.illustrationDescription: Spread the legs and set the tr

steps.instruction: Adjust cooking temperature by raising or lowering the pot.
steps.helpDetail: Use a hook and chain setup for easy height adjustment. Higher = lower heat, lower = higher heat.

steps.instruction: The tripod is the most stable camp cooking system.
steps.helpDetail: Unlike a spit, it won't tip over. Unlike a forked-stick setup, it stands independently.
steps.illustrationDescription: The tripod is the most stable

steps.instruction: Remove the pot using a sturdy stick through the bail handle.
steps.helpDetail: Metal pot handles become painfully hot. Always use a pot lifter or thick leather gloves.
steps.illustrationDescription: Remove the pot using a sturdy

techniques.name: Spit Roasting Technique
techniques.subtitle: Roasting meat on a rotating horizontal spit over fire

steps.instruction: Drive two forked stakes into the ground on either side of the fire.
steps.helpDetail: The forks should be at the same height, about 18 inches above the coals.
steps.illustrationDescription: Drive two forked stakes into t

steps.instruction: Skewer the meat lengthwise on a green, hardwood spit (avoid resinous pine).
steps.helpDetail: The spit must be strong enough not to bend under the weight of the meat.

steps.instruction: Rest the spit in the forks. Let gravity rotate it or turn it manually every 10 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Place a drip pan (flat rock, bark tray) beneath to catch fats for later use.
steps.illustrationDescription: Rest the spit in the forks. Le

steps.instruction: Cook over COALS, not flames.
steps.helpDetail: Flames char the outside while leaving the inside raw. A bed of hot, white coals provides steady, even heat.

steps.instruction: Meat is done when the juices run clear, not pink.
steps.helpDetail: Pierce the thickest part with a sharp stick. For wild game, always cook thoroughly to kill parasites.
steps.illustrationDescription: Meat is done when the juices r

techniques.name: Box Trap for Small Game
techniques.subtitle: Building a live-capture trap from sticks or a container

steps.instruction: Build a rectangular box from sticks lashed together, or use an existing container (bucket, crate).
steps.helpDetail: The trap must be strong enough that the animal cannot chew or claw its way out.
steps.illustrationDescription: Build a rectangular box from s

steps.instruction: Prop one end open with a stick connected to a trigger.
steps.helpDetail: The trigger should be a figure-4 or simple toggle that collapses when the bait is disturbed.
steps.illustrationDescription: Prop one end open with a stick

steps.instruction: Bait the trigger with food appropriate for the target animal.
steps.helpDetail: Peanut butter, seeds, fruit, or meat scraps depending on species.
steps.illustrationDescription: Bait the trigger with food app

steps.instruction: The animal enters, disturbs the trigger, and the propped end falls, trapping it inside.
steps.helpDetail: Live traps allow you to inspect the catch and release non-target species.

steps.instruction: Check box traps every 12 hours.
steps.helpDetail: Leaving an animal trapped longer causes it to dehydrate, overheat, or chew its way free.

techniques.name: Pit Trap with Stakes
techniques.subtitle: Constructing an in-ground fall trap for medium game

steps.instruction: Dig a pit 4 feet deep and 3 feet wide on a known game trail.
steps.helpDetail: Use the excavated dirt to fill and level any disturbed areas around the pit, making it undetectable.
steps.illustrationDescription: Dig a pit 4 feet deep and 3 fe

steps.instruction: Sharpen 4-6 hardwood stakes and drive them into the pit floor, points up.
steps.helpDetail: Fire-harden the tips for maximum penetration.

steps.instruction: Cover the pit with thin, breakable sticks laid across the opening.
steps.helpDetail: Layer leaves and natural ground cover on top to camouflage the trap completely.
steps.illustrationDescription: Cover the pit with thin, break

steps.instruction: Build funnel walls (brush barriers converging toward the pit) to guide animals over it.
steps.helpDetail: Animals follow the path of least resistance.

steps.instruction: Mark the pit clearly so you don't fall into your own trap.
steps.helpDetail: Use a private visual marker (rock pattern) that only you will recognize.
steps.illustrationDescription: Mark the pit clearly so you do

techniques.name: Channel Deadfall Trap
techniques.subtitle: A heavy stone trap guided by brush walls for ground animals

steps.instruction: Find or create a heavy, flat stone (20+ pounds).
steps.helpDetail: This is the kill weight. It must be heavy enough to dispatch the target animal instantly.
steps.illustrationDescription: Find or create a heavy, flat s

steps.instruction: Build two low brush walls creating a narrow channel leading to the trap.
steps.helpDetail: The channel should be just wide enough for the target animal.
steps.illustrationDescription: Build two low brush walls crea

steps.instruction: Prop the stone on a Figure-4 trigger.
steps.helpDetail: The trigger consists of three sticks notched to interlock: the upright, the diagonal, and the bait stick.
steps.illustrationDescription: Prop the stone on a Figure-4 t

steps.instruction: Bait the end of the bait stick.
steps.helpDetail: When the animal reaches the bait and pushes or pulls the stick, the Figure-4 collapses and the stone falls.
steps.illustrationDescription: Bait the end of the bait stick

steps.instruction: Place the trap on a hard surface (flat rock) for maximum impact.
steps.helpDetail: Soft ground absorbs the impact and may not kill the animal. Check traps every 12 hours.
steps.illustrationDescription: Place the trap on a hard surfa

techniques.name: Harvesting Inner Tree Bark (Cambium)
techniques.subtitle: Emergency winter starvation calories

steps.instruction: Identify an edible tree.
steps.helpDetail: Safe species include Pine, Birch, Willow, and Maple. The inner bark of pine is particularly nutritious.

steps.instruction: Score the outer bark.
steps.helpDetail: Using a knife or sharp rock, cut a square through the rough, dead outer bark.

steps.instruction: Peel back to the cambium layer.
steps.helpDetail: Peel off the outer bark. You are looking for the soft, moist, white/greenish layer directly against the hard wood.

steps.instruction: Scrape off the cambium.
steps.helpDetail: Scrape the sweet inner bark off the wood in strips. Do not girdle (cut entirely around) the tree, or you will kill it.

steps.instruction: Boil or fry to eat.
steps.helpDetail: Raw cambium is difficult to digest. Slice it into thin strips and boil it like noodles, or roast it until crispy.

techniques.name: Figure-4 Deadfall Bait Placement
techniques.subtitle: Optimizing trigger sensitivity and bait positioning

steps.instruction: Build trigger from three sticks
steps.helpDetail: Upright, diagonal, and bait stick. Notches must be precise.

steps.instruction: Place bait away from the back of the rock
steps.helpDetail: Animal must reach under the rock to access bait, ensuring the rock falls on it.

steps.instruction: Set trigger with minimal pressure
steps.helpDetail: The bait stick should barely support the weight.

steps.instruction: Check traps every 12 hours
steps.helpDetail: To prevent suffering and scavenging by other animals.

techniques.name: Tidal Pool Foraging
techniques.subtitle: Harvesting shellfish and sea creatures at low tide

steps.instruction: Time your visit to low tide
steps.helpDetail: Maximum exposure of pools occurs at spring low tides.

steps.instruction: Look for limpets, mussels, periwinkles
steps.helpDetail: Attached to rocks. Pry off with a knife or flat stone.

steps.instruction: Check for sea anemones and crabs
steps.helpDetail: Edible but must be cooked. Avoid anything brightly colored.

steps.instruction: Cook all tidal pool catches thoroughly
steps.helpDetail: Risk of paralytic shellfish poisoning in some regions.

techniques.name: Tree Bark Bread
techniques.subtitle: Making flour from inner bark of pine and birch

steps.instruction: Harvest inner bark (cambium layer)
steps.helpDetail: Strip the thin, moist layer between outer bark and wood.

steps.instruction: Dry completely
steps.helpDetail: Sun-dry or fire-dry until brittle.

steps.instruction: Grind to flour
steps.helpDetail: Pound with rocks or use improvised mortar and pestle.

steps.instruction: Mix with water and cook on hot stone
steps.helpDetail: The resulting cake is calorie-dense if bland.

techniques.name: Snare Set Optimization
techniques.subtitle: Maximizing catch rate with proper placement

steps.instruction: Set snares on active game trails
steps.helpDetail: Look for fresh tracks, droppings, and bent vegetation.

steps.instruction: Position loop at head height of target animal
steps.helpDetail: Rabbit: 4 inches off ground, 4 inch diameter loop.

steps.instruction: Funnel animal into snare
steps.helpDetail: Place guide sticks to narrow the path toward your snare.

steps.instruction: Set 10+ snares to increase odds
steps.helpDetail: One snare has low probability. Volume is everything.

techniques.name: Earth Oven (Hangi/Imu)
techniques.subtitle: Underground pit cooking for large quantities

steps.instruction: Dig a pit 2-3 feet deep
steps.helpDetail: Wide enough for your food quantity.

steps.instruction: Heat rocks in a fire for 2+ hours
steps.helpDetail: River rocks can explode—use dry, dense stones.

steps.instruction: Layer: hot rocks, green leaves, food, leaves, dirt
steps.helpDetail: The insulation traps heat and steam.

steps.instruction: Cook for 4-8 hours
steps.helpDetail: The result is slow-cooked, tender, and retains maximum nutrition.

techniques.name: Improvised Crayfish Trap
techniques.subtitle: Building a funnel trap from plastic bottles

steps.instruction: Cut a plastic bottle at the shoulder
steps.helpDetail: Keep both pieces.

steps.instruction: Invert the top into the bottom
steps.helpDetail: Creates a funnel entrance crayfish can enter but can't exit.

steps.instruction: Bait with fish scraps or meat
steps.helpDetail: Place bait at the bottom of the trap.

steps.instruction: Weight and submerge in stream
steps.helpDetail: Place in rocky areas with current. Check every few hours.

techniques.name: Wild Greens Identification Guide
techniques.subtitle: Safe common edible wild plants

steps.instruction: Dandelion
steps.helpDetail: Entire plant edible. Leaves, roots, flowers. Bitter but nutritious.

steps.instruction: Clover
steps.helpDetail: Flowers and leaves edible raw. High in protein for a plant.

steps.instruction: Chickweed
steps.helpDetail: Small white flowers, succulent leaves. Mild flavor, eat raw.

steps.instruction: Violets
steps.helpDetail: Flowers and leaves edible raw. High in vitamin C and A.

techniques.name: Primitive Jerky Making
techniques.subtitle: Preserving meat without modern equipment

steps.instruction: Slice meat into thin strips
steps.helpDetail: 1/4 inch thick, with the grain for chewing strips, against for tender.

steps.instruction: Salt heavily if salt is available
steps.helpDetail: Salt draws moisture and inhibits bacteria.

steps.instruction: Dry over low smoke for 6-12 hours
steps.helpDetail: Smoke adds antimicrobial compounds. Keep temperature below 160°F.

steps.instruction: Test doneness by bending
steps.helpDetail: Properly dried jerky cracks but doesn't break. Should not be moist inside.

techniques.name: Acorn Cold Water Leaching
techniques.subtitle: Removing tannins without fire

steps.instruction: Crack and shell acorns
steps.helpDetail: Remove the nutmeat from the shell.

steps.instruction: Grind to coarse meal
steps.helpDetail: Pound with rocks.

steps.instruction: Place in a mesh bag in running water
steps.helpDetail: A stream does the work for you. Takes 2-7 days.

steps.instruction: Test for bitterness
steps.helpDetail: When no longer astringent, dry and use as flour.

techniques.name: Cattail Multi-Use Harvesting
techniques.subtitle: Using every part of the cattail plant

steps.instruction: Root starch
steps.helpDetail: Dig up rhizomes, peel, and chew or dry and pound into flour. Available year-round.

steps.instruction: Young shoots
steps.helpDetail: In spring, the inner white shoot is edible raw. Tastes like cucumber.

steps.instruction: Pollen
steps.helpDetail: Shake mature male heads to collect yellow pollen. Add to flour for protein.

steps.instruction: Fluff for insulation/tinder
steps.helpDetail: Mature seed heads provide both excellent fire tinder and clothing insulation.

techniques.name: Water Hemlock Identification
techniques.subtitle: The deadliest plant in North America

steps.instruction: Grows in wet areas
steps.helpDetail: Streams, marshes, ditches. Often confused with edible plants.

steps.instruction: Hollow stem with purple streaks
steps.helpDetail: Cross-cut the root: chambered with yellowish oily liquid = FATAL.

steps.instruction: Symptoms appear in 15-30 minutes
steps.helpDetail: Violent seizures, then death. There is no antidote in the field.

steps.instruction: If ingested, induce vomiting immediately
steps.helpDetail: This is one of the few cases where induced vomiting may be life-saving.

techniques.name: Universal Edibility Test (Complete)
techniques.subtitle: 8-hour systematic testing protocol

steps.instruction: Test one plant part at a time
steps.helpDetail: Leaf, stem, root, flower SEPARATELY. One may be edible while another is toxic.

steps.instruction: Skin contact test (8 hours)
steps.helpDetail: Rub plant on inner wrist. Wait for reaction.

steps.instruction: Lip and tongue test
steps.helpDetail: Touch to lip corner, wait 15 min. Touch to tongue tip, wait 15 min. Chew and spit, wait 15 min.

steps.instruction: Eat small amount and wait 8 hours
steps.helpDetail: If no ill effects after 8 hours with no other food, the plant part is safe.

techniques.name: Bird Snaring Techniques
techniques.subtitle: Ground-based snare sets for upland birds

steps.instruction: Identify bird feeding areas
steps.helpDetail: Look for scratched ground, seeds, droppings.

steps.instruction: Set multiple small snares in a grid
steps.helpDetail: Use thin mono-filament or fine wire. Small loops at ground level.

steps.instruction: Bait the center of the grid
steps.helpDetail: Scatter seeds or berries to attract birds to the snare area.

steps.instruction: Check frequently
steps.helpDetail: Birds can damage themselves quickly in snares.

techniques.name: Fish Stunning with Natural Compounds
techniques.subtitle: Using plant-derived compounds to harvest fish

steps.instruction: Identify rotenone-containing plants
steps.helpDetail: Mullein seeds, green walnut husks, or dried fish bean pods.

steps.instruction: Crush and release into still water
steps.helpDetail: Dams or pools work best. Moving water dilutes the compound.

steps.instruction: Fish float to surface stunned
steps.helpDetail: They suffocate temporarily. Collect them quickly.

steps.instruction: This is a survival-only technique
steps.helpDetail: Illegal in most jurisdictions. The compound is harmless to humans when fish is cooked.

techniques.name: Chicory Root Coffee
techniques.subtitle: Caffeine-free hot drink from a common roadside plant

steps.instruction: Identify: blue daisy-like flowers, basal rosette of dandelion-like toothed leaves.
steps.helpDetail: Extremely common along roadsides and fields worldwide.

steps.instruction: Dig roots — they're long, thick taproots.
steps.helpDetail: Best harvested in fall when roots store maximum energy.

steps.instruction: Clean, chop into small pieces, and roast until dark brown and fragrant.
steps.helpDetail: Roast slowly over low heat. They should snap when fully dried.

steps.instruction: Grind roasted pieces and steep in boiling water like coffee.
steps.helpDetail: Tastes remarkably like coffee. Used as coffee substitute since Napoleonic Wars. Roots also edible boiled as a vegetable.

techniques.name: Burdock Root
techniques.subtitle: Nutritious root vegetable — common worldwide weed

steps.instruction: Identify: large rhubarb-like leaves, sticky burrs, thick stem.
steps.helpDetail: First-year plants have no flower stalk — these have the best roots.

steps.instruction: Dig the root — it can be 2+ feet deep.
steps.helpDetail: Long, brown, carrot-like root. Best harvested in fall or early spring.

steps.instruction: Peel and slice thinly.
steps.helpDetail: The peel is bitter. Inner root is white and starchy.

steps.instruction: Boil, roast, or eat raw. Tastes like a mild potato.
steps.helpDetail: High in inulin (prebiotic fiber), iron, and potassium. Eaten daily in Japan as 'gobo'.

techniques.name: Lamb's Quarters (Wild Spinach)
techniques.subtitle: Most nutritious wild green — better than store spinach

steps.instruction: Identify: diamond-shaped leaves with white powdery coating underneath.
steps.helpDetail: Grows in disturbed soil, gardens, edges of fields. Worldwide distribution.

steps.instruction: Harvest young leaves and growing tips.
steps.helpDetail: Older leaves become tough and accumulate oxalates.

steps.instruction: Eat raw in salads or cook like spinach.
steps.helpDetail: Blanching reduces oxalic acid content.

steps.instruction: More protein, calcium, and iron than domestic spinach.
steps.helpDetail: The single most important wild green for caloric survival. Seeds can also be ground into flour.

techniques.name: Wood Sorrel
techniques.subtitle: Tangy lemony green — thirst-quenching and vitamin-rich

steps.instruction: Identify: shamrock-shaped leaves (three heart-shaped leaflets), small yellow or pink flowers.
steps.helpDetail: NOT clover — wood sorrel leaflets are heart-shaped. Clover leaflets are round.

steps.instruction: Taste a leaf — it's distinctly sour/lemony.
steps.helpDetail: The tangy flavor is from oxalic acid. Safe to eat but don't consume massive quantities.

steps.instruction: Eat raw. All parts are edible.
steps.helpDetail: Leaves, stems, flowers, and seed pods.

steps.instruction: Excellent for seasoning bland survival foods and quenching thirst.
steps.helpDetail: High in vitamin C. The sour taste stimulates saliva production, reducing perceived thirst.

techniques.name: Jerusalem Artichoke (Sunchoke)
techniques.subtitle: High-calorie tuber — no planting required

steps.instruction: Identify: tall sunflower-like plant with small yellow flowers.
steps.helpDetail: Grows in colonies along roadsides and stream banks. 6-10 feet tall.

steps.instruction: Dig tubers from the root mass — looks like knobby ginger.
steps.helpDetail: Harvest after first frost for best flavor. Tubers are white to purple.

steps.instruction: Scrub clean. Can eat raw (nutty/crunchy) or cooked.
steps.helpDetail: Roasting caramelizes the inulin sugars, making them sweeter.

steps.instruction: High calorie density — one of the best wild calorie sources.
steps.helpDetail: Warning: contains inulin which causes gas in people not accustomed to it. Start with small amounts.

techniques.name: Deer Track Identification & Trailing
techniques.subtitle: Follow deer to food, water, and predict movements

steps.instruction: Identify tracks: two-toed heart-shaped prints, 2-3 inches long.
steps.helpDetail: Dew claws show in deep mud or when running. Walking stride: 18-22 inches.

steps.instruction: Read trail signs: browse lines on vegetation, rubs on trees, droppings.
steps.helpDetail: Fresh droppings are shiny and soft. Rubs indicate rutting activity.

steps.instruction: Follow trails to feeding areas (forest edges) and watering spots.
steps.helpDetail: Deer travel the same trails repeatedly. Morning: food to bedding. Evening: bedding to food.

steps.instruction: Set snares or blinds along trails, NOT at water sources.
steps.helpDetail: Ethics and effectiveness: trail snares have higher success rates than water source ambushes.

techniques.name: Rabbit Track Identification & Habits
techniques.subtitle: Most common small game — year-round availability

steps.instruction: Identify tracks: two large hind prints ahead of two small front prints in a Y pattern.
steps.helpDetail: Rabbits hop — hind feet land AHEAD of front feet. Fresh tracks in mud or snow are clear.

steps.instruction: Find runs: well-worn paths under brush, along fence lines, and between cover areas.
steps.helpDetail: Rabbits use the same paths daily. Runs are narrow tunnels through thick vegetation.

steps.instruction: Locate warrens and forms (resting spots) — look for clusters of droppings.
steps.helpDetail: Pellet clusters mark territory and indicate active areas.

steps.instruction: Set snares at runs using slip knots at head height (3-4 inches off ground).
steps.helpDetail: Best set at chokepoints where the run passes under a log or through a gap. Check snares twice daily.

techniques.name: Bird Observation for Food & Weather
techniques.subtitle: Birds reveal food sources, water, and weather changes

steps.instruction: Follow birds to water: morning flights converge toward water sources.
steps.helpDetail: Pigeons, doves, and finches fly DIRECTLY to water at dawn and dusk. Follow their flight lines.

steps.instruction: Watch for ground-feeding birds (quail, grouse, turkeys) to find seed and berry sources.
steps.helpDetail: Where they eat, you can eat. Mark locations for return foraging trips.

steps.instruction: Read weather from bird behavior: birds going quiet = storm approaching.
steps.helpDetail: Low-flying swallows indicate low pressure (insects fly lower). Birds roosting mid-day = severe weather incoming.

steps.instruction: Hawks circling may indicate meadows with rodent populations — set traps there.
steps.helpDetail: Raptor hunting grounds = high prey density = productive trapping areas.

techniques.name: Underground Pit Roasting
techniques.subtitle: Slow-cook large quantities of food with no pots

steps.instruction: Dig a pit 2 feet deep and 3 feet wide. Line bottom with flat rocks.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT use river rocks — trapped moisture causes them to explode when heated.

steps.instruction: Build a large fire in the pit and burn for 2+ hours until rocks are superheated.
steps.helpDetail: You need a deep bed of red-hot coals and rocks glowing with heat.

steps.instruction: Remove excess coals. Lay wet grass/leaves on rocks, add food, more wet vegetation on top.
steps.helpDetail: The wet vegetation creates steam. Wrap food in large leaves if available.

steps.instruction: Cover with earth and let cook for 3-8 hours depending on quantity.
steps.helpDetail: This cooks entire animals, root vegetables, and tubers. The Polynesian 'imu' and New England clambake use this method.

techniques.name: Ash Roasting (Direct Coal Cooking)
techniques.subtitle: Cook directly in coals — simplest cooking method

steps.instruction: Create a bed of hot coals (NOT flames) from a mature fire.
steps.helpDetail: Rake coals to one side. You want even heat, not direct flame.

steps.instruction: Wrap food in large green leaves or clay.
steps.helpDetail: Clay coating seals in moisture and peels away taking feathers/scales with it.

steps.instruction: Bury wrapped food in hot coals and cover.
steps.helpDetail: Push coals over and around the food packet.

steps.instruction: Cook time: fish 15-20 min, vegetables 20-30 min, game birds 45-60 min.
steps.helpDetail: Check by opening packaging. Meat should be white throughout. This method works without any tools or utensils.

techniques.name: Fish Smoking — Field Preservation
techniques.subtitle: Hot-smoke fish for 1-2 weeks of storage

steps.instruction: Clean and split fish — remove guts, open flat, score thick portions.
steps.helpDetail: Leave skin on. Remove head or not — personal preference.

steps.instruction: Salt heavily if salt available. If not, proceed directly to smoking.
steps.helpDetail: Salt draws moisture and adds preservation. 1 hour of salt brining helps significantly.

steps.instruction: Build a smoking rack: green stick frame 2-3 feet above a smoky fire.
steps.helpDetail: Use hardwood (oak, hickory, alder, maple). AVOID softwoods (pine, spruce) — resin makes it bitter and can be toxic.

steps.instruction: Maintain steady smoke (not flames) for 4-8 hours. Fish should be firm and golden.
steps.helpDetail: Hot smoking cooks and preserves simultaneously. Properly smoked fish keeps 1-2 weeks without refrigeration. Cold smoking (requires separate fire and chimney) extends this to months.

techniques.name: Spring Snare (Trigger Snare)
techniques.subtitle: Auto-lifting snare that cleanly catches small game

steps.instruction: Find a flexible sapling near an active trail.
steps.helpDetail: Should bend to the ground easily and spring back strongly when released.

steps.instruction: Carve trigger: two interlocking hooks (notch-and-peg system).
steps.helpDetail: One peg is staked in the ground, the other attached to the bent sapling. They hook under tension.

steps.instruction: Attach snare loop to the sapling end. Set the trigger under tension.
steps.helpDetail: The slightest pull on the snare line should release the trigger.

steps.instruction: Bait around the snare or set on a funnel point of a well-used trail.
steps.helpDetail: When triggered, the sapling snaps upright, lifting the catch off the ground — protecting it from ground predators. Check every 8-12 hours.

techniques.name: Improvised Fish Hook
techniques.subtitle: Make hooks from thorns, bone, safety pins, or wire

steps.instruction: Select material: thorn, bone shard, safety pin, soda can tab, wire, or wood.
steps.helpDetail: Thorns from hawthorn or honey locust are nature's fish hooks.

steps.instruction: Shape into a hook: curve with a sharp point and a barb if possible.
steps.helpDetail: Bone: carve into a gorge hook (straight, sharpened both ends). Wire: bend into J-shape.

steps.instruction: Attach to line (cordage, thread, dental floss, or unraveled paracord inner strand).
steps.helpDetail: Tie securely — every lost hook is lost opportunity.

steps.instruction: Bait with insects, worms, or small pieces of meat. Set in calm water near structure.
steps.helpDetail: Gorge hooks work differently: fish swallows the whole thing, and it turns sideways when you pull. No setting motion needed.

techniques.name: Gill Net Construction
techniques.subtitle: Passive fishing — catches fish while you do other tasks

steps.instruction: Determine mesh size by target fish: 2-inch mesh for panfish, 4-inch for larger.
steps.helpDetail: Mesh is measured as stretched diagonal. Fish swim into the net and their gills get caught.

steps.instruction: Tie net-making knots (sheet bends) using a mesh gauge stick for consistent spacing.
steps.helpDetail: Start with a top cord (headline). Tie loops at equal intervals.

steps.instruction: Build net 3-4 feet deep and as wide as you can manage.
steps.helpDetail: Even a 10-foot wide net catches fish. Wider is better.

steps.instruction: Set across current in rivers or perpendicular to shore in lakes. Weight the bottom, float the top.
steps.helpDetail: Use stones as weights and bark/wood as floats. Check every 2-4 hours. Passive fishing lets you work on other survival tasks.

techniques.name: Edible Insect Field Guide
techniques.subtitle: High-protein emergency food — most insects are safe

steps.instruction: Safe to eat: grasshoppers, crickets, ants, termites, grubs, beetle larvae.
steps.helpDetail: Rule: avoid brightly colored insects, hairy ones, and anything that stings. Bright colors = toxin warning.

steps.instruction: ALWAYS cook insects — heat kills parasites.
steps.helpDetail: Roast on a flat rock, boil, or skewer and roast over flame.

steps.instruction: Remove legs and wings from grasshoppers/crickets before eating.
steps.helpDetail: The spiny legs can lodge in your throat. Bodies are the nutritious part.

steps.instruction: Protein content: 60-70% by dry weight — higher than beef.
steps.helpDetail: 100g of crickets = 121 calories, 12g protein. Termite mounds provide thousands of calories. 80% of world cultures eat insects.

techniques.name: Earthworm Harvesting & Preparation
techniques.subtitle: Available everywhere, year-round, high protein

steps.instruction: Find in moist soil: under rocks, rotting logs, or dig in rich forest floor.
steps.helpDetail: After rain, worms surface — easy collection.

steps.instruction: Purge: place worms in damp grass for 24 hours to empty their digestive tracts.
steps.helpDetail: This removes soil and grit. Improves taste significantly.

steps.instruction: Kill by dropping into boiling water. Boil 3-5 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Alternatively: dry in sun and grind into protein powder.

steps.instruction: Eat whole or add to stews. 60% protein, rich in iron.
steps.helpDetail: Taste is mild, slightly earthy. Frying improves flavor. Dried worm powder stores well and can supplement any meal.

techniques.name: Dandelion Complete Harvest
techniques.subtitle: Every part edible — leaves, roots, and flowers

steps.instruction: Identify: jagged-tooth leaves in a rosette, hollow stem with white sap, yellow flower.
steps.helpDetail: One of the most common and recognizable plants on Earth. Grows on every continent except Antarctica.

steps.instruction: Young leaves: eat raw in salads. Older leaves: boil to reduce bitterness.
steps.helpDetail: Harvest young spring leaves for best flavor. More nutritious than store-bought spinach.

steps.instruction: Roots: dig, clean, roast until dark brown, grind for coffee substitute.
steps.helpDetail: Dandelion root coffee is surprisingly good. Earthy, slightly bitter.

steps.instruction: Flowers: eat raw, batter and fry, or make wine.
steps.helpDetail: Complete nutritional profile: vitamins A, C, K, B6, calcium, iron, potassium. The ultimate survival plant.

techniques.name: Red & White Clover Foraging
techniques.subtitle: Common lawn plant — entirely edible and nutritious

steps.instruction: Identify: three round leaflets, white or red-purple ball-shaped flowers.
steps.helpDetail: Grows in lawns, meadows, roadsides worldwide. Very hard to misidentify.

steps.instruction: Flowers: eat raw, dried for tea, or cooked.
steps.helpDetail: Flowers are the tastiest part. Sweet, mild flavor.

steps.instruction: Leaves: best cooked or dried — raw is tough to digest in quantity.
steps.helpDetail: Boiling for 5-10 minutes breaks down the tough fibers.

steps.instruction: Seeds and dried flower heads can be ground into flour.
steps.helpDetail: Clover is a nitrogen-fixer — enriches soil. High in protein for a plant. Clover tea is traditionally used for colds and coughs.

techniques.name: Rose Hip Harvest & Use
techniques.subtitle: Vitamin C powerhouse — available through winter

steps.instruction: Identify: red/orange seed pods that remain on rose bushes after petals fall.
steps.helpDetail: Available September through March. Sweeter after first frost.

steps.instruction: Harvest by picking — they pull off easily when ripe.
steps.helpDetail: Wear gloves if the bush has thorns. Collect in quantity.

steps.instruction: Cut in half, scrape out seeds and fine hairs with a spoon.
steps.helpDetail: The hairs inside are irritating to the digestive tract. Remove them.

steps.instruction: Eat raw, brew into tea, or make syrup by boiling and straining.
steps.helpDetail: Contains 20x more vitamin C than oranges by weight. British WWII ration supplement. Prevents scurvy in extended survival.

techniques.name: Wild Mushroom Safety Rules
techniques.subtitle: Critical rules before foraging ANY mushroom

steps.instruction: NEVER eat a mushroom you cannot positively identify with 100% certainty.
steps.helpDetail: There is no universal test for mushroom safety. No color test, no silver spoon test, no animal-feeding test works.

steps.instruction: Start with foolproof species: morels, chicken of the woods, giant puffball.
steps.helpDetail: These have NO deadly look-alikes and are easy to identify positively.

steps.instruction: Deadly species to memorize: Death Cap, Destroying Angel, Galerina.
steps.helpDetail: Death Cap causes 90% of mushroom fatalities worldwide. Looks innocuous — greenish cap, white gills, ring on stem.

steps.instruction: When in doubt, DON'T EAT IT. No mushroom calorie is worth the risk of liver failure.
steps.helpDetail: Mushroom poisoning can have 6-24 hour delayed symptoms — you feel fine then your liver shuts down. By then it's too late.

techniques.name: Basket Fish Trap (Funnel Trap)
techniques.subtitle: Passive fish catcher — works while you sleep

steps.instruction: Weave a cone-shaped basket from flexible branches (willow, vine).
steps.helpDetail: Large end: 18 inches diameter. Taper to a point. Total length: 2-3 feet.

steps.instruction: Create a funnel entrance: a second smaller cone that fits inside the opening.
steps.helpDetail: Fish swim through the funnel into the basket but can't figure out how to swim back through the narrow opening.

steps.instruction: Bait with crushed insects, bread, or meat scraps inside the trap.
steps.helpDetail: Place bait at the far end from the entrance.

steps.instruction: Set in stream facing downstream, or in still water near shore. Weight down with rocks.
steps.helpDetail: Check every 4-8 hours. Works 24/7 with no active attention required. Multiple traps multiply your catch.

techniques.name: Solar Food Dehydrator (Field Build)
techniques.subtitle: Sun-dry food for long-term storage without electricity

steps.instruction: Build a frame: 3 feet wide, 4 feet long, with a mesh bottom (screen, cloth, or woven grass).
steps.helpDetail: Angle the frame toward the sun at about 30 degrees.

steps.instruction: Slice food thin: 1/4 inch strips for meat, thin slices for fruit and vegetables.
steps.helpDetail: Uniform thickness ensures even drying. Thinner = faster.

steps.instruction: Place on mesh. Cover with a second mesh layer to keep insects off.
steps.helpDetail: Leave gaps for airflow. Air circulation is as important as heat.

steps.instruction: Dry for 1-3 days depending on humidity and food type. Food is done when leathery (fruit) or brittle (meat).
steps.helpDetail: Properly dried food stores for weeks to months at room temperature. In dry climates, jerky can last a year.

techniques.name: Salt Extraction from Natural Sources
techniques.subtitle: Essential mineral for survival — extract from soil, plants, or seawater

steps.instruction: Seawater: boil in a wide pan until all water evaporates — salt remains.
steps.helpDetail: 1 gallon of seawater yields ~4.5 ounces of salt. Use a flat rock as a pan.

steps.instruction: Salt licks: scrape salt deposits from mineral deposits. Animals will lead you to them.
steps.helpDetail: Follow deer and elk trails to natural salt licks. Scrape the crusted mineral.

steps.instruction: Hickory bark method: burn hickory bark, dissolve ash in water, evaporate water.
steps.helpDetail: The resulting crystal is potassium-rich salt substitute (similar to Nu-Salt).

steps.instruction: Salt is critical for: food preservation, electrolyte balance, hide tanning, and seasoning.
steps.helpDetail: A person needs 1-2 grams of sodium daily. In hot climates with heavy sweating: 3-5 grams. Deficiency causes muscle cramps, confusion, and eventually death.

techniques.name: Wild Grape Identification
techniques.subtitle: Sweet fruit plus leaves are edible

steps.instruction: Identify: climbing vine with tendrils, toothed heart-shaped leaves, fruit in clusters.
steps.helpDetail: Found woodland edges and clearings across North America. Bark shreds in strips.

steps.instruction: Check for look-alikes: moonseed has no tendrils and single seed. Canada moonseed is toxic.
steps.helpDetail: True grapes: multiple seeds per fruit, tendrils present, bark peels in strips.

steps.instruction: Harvest ripe fruit (dark purple/blue) in late summer through fall.
steps.helpDetail: Green grapes are extremely sour. Let them fully ripen.

steps.instruction: Eat raw, dry as raisins, or ferment. Young leaves edible stuffed and cooked.
steps.helpDetail: High in sugar and vitamin C. Dried grape raisins store for months.

techniques.name: Pawpaw Fruit Harvest
techniques.subtitle: Tropical-tasting fruit native to eastern North America

steps.instruction: Identify: small understory tree, large tropical-looking leaves 6-12 inches long.
steps.helpDetail: Grows in rich bottomlands, eastern US. Leaves smell pungent when crushed.

steps.instruction: Fruit ripens in September — green turning yellow-brown, feels soft like a ripe avocado.
steps.helpDetail: Falls from tree when ripe. Check ground under trees daily during season.

steps.instruction: Eat the custard-like flesh raw. Discard large brown seeds.
steps.helpDetail: Tastes like banana-mango custard. Seriously.

steps.instruction: Cannot be stored long — eat within 2-3 days of ripening.
steps.helpDetail: Highest calorie native North American fruit. 80 calories per fruit. Freeze-dry or make into leather for preservation.

techniques.name: Hickory Nut Harvest & Cracking
techniques.subtitle: High-calorie nut available across eastern forests

steps.instruction: Identify: compound leaves with 5-7 leaflets, thick-shelled round nuts in husks.
steps.helpDetail: Shagbark and shellbark hickory have the best-tasting nuts. Bitternut hickory is bitter.

steps.instruction: Harvest fallen nuts in autumn. Remove green husks.
steps.helpDetail: Hulls split when ripe. Nuts inside are extremely hard-shelled.

steps.instruction: Crack with a rock on a flat stone anvil. Pick out nut meat with a thorn or pin.
steps.helpDetail: Hit at the seam. Patience required — shells are among the hardest in nature.

steps.instruction: Eat raw or roast. Grind into nut butter. Boil shells for hickory nut milk.
steps.helpDetail: Over 150 calories per ounce — one of the highest calorie foraged foods. Hickory nut oil is excellent for cooking.

techniques.name: Black Walnut Processing
techniques.subtitle: Powerful-flavored nut requiring hull removal

steps.instruction: Identify: compound leaves with 15-23 leaflets, round green-hulled nuts.
steps.helpDetail: Black walnut trees are common in eastern North America. Stain everything dark brown.

steps.instruction: Remove green hulls: stomp with boots or drive over with vehicle.
steps.helpDetail: Wear gloves — hull juice permanently stains skin brown. Let hulls rot off after several days.

steps.instruction: Crack dried nuts with a hammer on concrete or flat rock.
steps.helpDetail: Hardest North American nut shell. Extract meat with a pick or nail.

steps.instruction: Rich, intense flavor. Use sparingly in cooking or eat straight.
steps.helpDetail: High in omega-3 fatty acids and protein. Hull dye is a powerful natural dark brown dye and antiparasitic.

techniques.name: Frog Hunting & Preparation
techniques.subtitle: Night-time protein harvest from any pond or marsh

steps.instruction: Hunt at night: frogs are active and visible with any light source (firelight, flashlight).
steps.helpDetail: Their eyes reflect light — scan pond edges slowly. They freeze when illuminated.

steps.instruction: Catch by hand: approach slowly from behind, grab firmly around the body.
steps.helpDetail: Or use a sharpened stick to gig (spear) larger bullfrogs.

steps.instruction: Skin by cutting around the waist, pulling skin off like pants.
steps.helpDetail: Remove head, skin, and guts. Only eat the legs and back.

steps.instruction: Cook legs simply: roast over fire or boil 5 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Taste mild, similar to chicken. Two large bullfrog legs = one serving. Rich in protein and potassium.

techniques.name: Field Jerky — Meat Drying Without Tools
techniques.subtitle: Preserve any meat for weeks using only sun and smoke

steps.instruction: Slice meat WITH the grain into thin strips, 1/4 inch thick maximum.
steps.helpDetail: Cutting with the grain makes tougher jerky that stores better. Remove all fat — fat goes rancid.

steps.instruction: Salt heavily if salt available. If not, smoke will preserve alone.
steps.helpDetail: Salt draws moisture and inhibits bacterial growth. Coat both sides.

steps.instruction: Hang strips on a drying rack in direct sun with constant airflow.
steps.helpDetail: Or lay on a rock in sun. Flip every 2 hours. Must be off the ground (insects).

steps.instruction: Properly dried jerky snaps when bent and has no moist spots.
steps.helpDetail: Stores 1-4 weeks at room temperature. In cool, dry conditions: months. Smoking adds antimicrobial compounds AND flavor.

techniques.name: Universal Edibility Test (UET)
techniques.subtitle: Systematically test unknown plants for safety — last resort only

steps.instruction: Fast for 8 hours. Test only ONE plant part at a time (leaf, stem, root separately).
steps.helpDetail: Each plant part may have different toxicity. Test individually.

steps.instruction: Skin contact test: crush plant part, rub on inner wrist. Wait 15 minutes for reaction.
steps.helpDetail: Burning, rash, numbness, or swelling = reject. Move to next candidate.

steps.instruction: Lip test → tongue tip test → small bite (chew, don't swallow) → wait 8 hours.
steps.helpDetail: Each stage: wait for adverse reaction before proceeding. Any negative = stop and try different plant.

steps.instruction: If no reaction after 8 hours: eat a small handful. Wait 8 more hours. If fine: plant part is likely safe.
steps.helpDetail: This test is LAST RESORT — it takes 24+ hours per plant part and is not 100% reliable. Knowledge of known plants is always superior.

techniques.name: Bow Fishing (Improvised)
techniques.subtitle: Shoot fish in shallow water with a modified bow

steps.instruction: Modify an arrow: attach a line to the shaft behind the point.
steps.helpDetail: The line retrieves both arrow and fish. Without the line, you lose your arrow in the water.

steps.instruction: Aim BELOW the fish — water refraction makes fish appear higher than they are.
steps.helpDetail: The deeper the fish, the more you must aim below. At 1 foot deep, aim 4 inches below.

steps.instruction: Shoot at fish in shallow, clear water — ideally at pools where they congregate.
steps.helpDetail: Murky water = no target. Swift current = missed shots. Find calm, clear pools.

steps.instruction: Retrieve arrow by pulling line. Re-use arrows — making them is time-consuming.
steps.helpDetail: Best for large, slow fish: carp, catfish, gar. Not effective for small, fast species.

techniques.name: Wild Berry Safety Rules
techniques.subtitle: Quick ID framework for safe berry foraging

steps.instruction: White and yellow berries: 90% are poisonous. AVOID unless positively identified.
steps.helpDetail: White baneberry, white snakeroot — most white berries are toxic.

steps.instruction: Blue and black berries: majority are safe (blueberry, blackberry, elderberry).
steps.helpDetail: Notable exception: pokeweed berries (dark purple, drooping clusters) are toxic.

steps.instruction: Red berries: 50/50 — some safe (strawberry, raspberry), some deadly (holly, yew).
steps.helpDetail: Red berries require individual identification. Don't gamble.

steps.instruction: Aggregate berries (like blackberry/raspberry structure) are almost always safe to eat.
steps.helpDetail: If a berry has the bumpy aggregate structure of a blackberry, it's nearly universally safe. This is one of the most reliable rules in foraging.

techniques.name: Bamboo Shoot Harvesting
techniques.subtitle: Abundant tropical/subtropical food source

steps.instruction: Identify bamboo shoots: emerging spears at the base of bamboo groves, under 12 inches tall.
steps.helpDetail: Harvest young, just-emerging shoots. Once they get woody, they're inedible.

steps.instruction: Peel outer sheaths layer by layer to reveal the tender inner core.
steps.helpDetail: Multiple tough layers surround the edible heart.

steps.instruction: Boil in water for 20-30 minutes to remove bitter compounds (cyanogenic glycosides).
steps.helpDetail: RAW bamboo shoots contain toxins. Always boil. Change water once during cooking for very bitter species.

steps.instruction: Eat boiled, stir-fried, or added to soups. Mild, slightly sweet flavor.
steps.helpDetail: 200+ species of bamboo are edible. Major food source in Asia. High in fiber, potassium, and low in calories.

techniques.name: Wild Rice Harvesting
techniques.subtitle: Aquatic grain — high calorie, grows in shallow lakes

steps.instruction: Identify: tall grass growing in shallow water with grain heads drooping over the surface.
steps.helpDetail: Found in lakes, rivers, and marshes across North America. Seeds ripen in late summer.

steps.instruction: Harvest from a canoe or by wading: bend stalks over and knock seeds into a container.
steps.helpDetail: Traditional harvesting uses two sticks — one to bend, one to knock.

steps.instruction: Dry the harvested rice in sun or over low heat.
