steps.helpDetail: Spread thin, turn frequently. Must be completely dry for storage.

steps.instruction: Parch in a dry pan over fire, then thresh to remove husks.
steps.helpDetail: Boil 30-40 minutes to cook. Wild rice has more protein and fiber than white rice. Stores for years when properly dried.

techniques.name: Pine Nut Harvesting
techniques.subtitle: High-calorie seed from specific pine species

steps.instruction: Identify pine species with large cones: pinyon, stone pine, Korean pine, Swiss pine.
steps.helpDetail: Not all pines have harvestable nuts. Look for thick, heavy cones.

steps.instruction: Harvest cones in fall when scales begin to open.
steps.helpDetail: Green cones: heat over fire to open scales and release seeds.

steps.instruction: Extract seeds from between the cone scales.
steps.helpDetail: Tedious but rewarding — each cone yields 20-50 seeds.

steps.instruction: Eat raw or roasted. Shell the hard outer coating to access the rich nut meat.
steps.helpDetail: 31 grams of fat per 100g — one of the most calorie-dense wild foods. Pine nuts have sustained indigenous peoples for millennia.

techniques.name: Field Maple Syrup Production
techniques.subtitle: Tap maple trees for sugar water and boil to syrup

steps.instruction: Identify sugar maple: opposite branching, 5-lobed leaves. Can also use any maple, birch, or walnut.
steps.helpDetail: Tap in late winter/early spring when nights are below freezing and days above.

steps.instruction: Drill or carve a hole 2 inches deep, 3 feet up the trunk. Insert a tube or carved spout.
steps.helpDetail: Angle slightly downward. Sap flows by gravity. Hang a container below.

steps.instruction: Collect sap — 1-3 gallons per day per tap. The sap is clear and slightly sweet.
steps.helpDetail: Raw sap is drinkable and slightly sweet. It's 2-3% sugar.

steps.instruction: Boil sap down: 40 gallons of sap = 1 gallon of syrup.
steps.helpDetail: Long, slow boil over an open fire. The syrup is done when it reaches 219°F. Incredible calorie source and morale booster.

techniques.name: Cactus Fruit and Pad Preparation
techniques.subtitle: Desert food and water source

steps.instruction: Prickly pear pads (nopales): use tongs or a stick to remove. Burn off spines over flame.
steps.helpDetail: Scrape remaining spine bases with a knife.

steps.instruction: Slice pads into strips. Boil or grill until tender (10-15 min).
steps.helpDetail: The mucilaginous (slimy) texture reduces with cooking.

steps.instruction: Prickly pear fruit (tunas): pick with gloved hands when red/purple. Peel skin.
steps.helpDetail: Roll on ground to remove tiny glochid spines first.

steps.instruction: Fruit is sweet and juicy — eat raw. Pads provide moisture and nutrition.
steps.helpDetail: Cactus provides both food AND water in the desert. Other edible cacti: barrel cactus (emergency water), dragon fruit.

techniques.name: Rabbit Stick (Throwing Stick)
techniques.subtitle: Simple hunting weapon for small game

steps.instruction: Select a dense hardwood stick: 2 feet long, slightly curved, forearm-thick.
steps.helpDetail: Oak, hickory, or any dense wood. Weight is important for kinetic energy.

steps.instruction: Flatten one or both ends slightly for aerodynamics.
steps.helpDetail: A slight flat helps it fly true.

steps.instruction: Throw sidearm at ground-level targets from 15-30 feet.
steps.helpDetail: Aim for the head/neck area. The stick tumbles end-over-end, covering a wide striking zone.

steps.instruction: Practice extensively before hunting — accuracy improves dramatically with repetition.
steps.helpDetail: The Australian boomerang started as a throwing stick. This is humanity's oldest projectile weapon. Effective against rabbits, squirrels, grouse, and other small game.

techniques.name: Ant Foraging as Emergency Protein
techniques.subtitle: Harvest and prepare ants for consumption

steps.instruction: Locate ant colonies: mounds, rotting logs, under rocks.
steps.helpDetail: Avoid fire ants and bullet ants. Most common black and brown ant species are safe.

steps.instruction: Use a stick inserted into the colony — ants climb up and can be swept into a container.
steps.helpDetail: Or dig into the colony and collect with a cup. Work quickly before they disperse.

steps.instruction: Kill by immersing in water or freezing. Some species can be eaten raw.
steps.helpDetail: Cooking improves flavor and kills any parasites.

steps.instruction: Roast on a flat stone for a crunchy, slightly sour snack.
steps.helpDetail: Formic acid gives ants a lemony taste. Carpenter ants are among the best-tasting. High in protein and iron.

techniques.name: Agave Heart Roasting
techniques.subtitle: Cooked agave heart is sweet and calorie-dense

steps.instruction: Find mature agave — thick leaves, central stalk emerging.
steps.helpDetail: Find mature agave — thick leaves, central stalk emerging.

steps.instruction: Cut leaves off to expose the heart (piña).
steps.helpDetail: Cut leaves off to expose the heart (piña).

steps.instruction: Pit-roast for 24-48 hours until caramelized and soft.
steps.helpDetail: Pit-roast for 24-48 hours until caramelized and soft.

steps.instruction: Rich in carbohydrates and sugars when cooked.
steps.helpDetail: Sustained indigenous peoples in the Sonoran desert for millennia.

techniques.name: Mesquite Pod Flour
techniques.subtitle: High-protein flour from desert tree pods

steps.instruction: Harvest ripe (dry, rattling) pods from mesquite trees in late summer.
steps.helpDetail: Harvest ripe (dry, rattling) pods from mesquite trees in late summer.

steps.instruction: Dry pods completely in sun — must be crispy.
steps.helpDetail: Dry pods completely in sun — must be crispy.

steps.instruction: Grind in a mortar or between stones into fine flour.
steps.helpDetail: Grind in a mortar or between stones into fine flour.

steps.instruction: 35% sugar, 11% protein — makes sweet flatbread.
steps.helpDetail: Mesquite flour is gluten-free and was a staple food of desert peoples.

techniques.name: Barrel Cactus Water Extraction
techniques.subtitle: Emergency water from the desert's water tank

steps.instruction: Carefully cut the top off a barrel cactus — avoid the spines.
steps.helpDetail: Carefully cut the top off a barrel cactus — avoid the spines.

steps.instruction: Mash the pulp inside to release moisture into the cavity.
steps.helpDetail: Mash the pulp inside to release moisture into the cavity.

steps.instruction: Strain liquid through cloth — it's bitter but drinkable.
steps.helpDetail: Strain liquid through cloth — it's bitter but drinkable.

steps.instruction: Use only as LAST RESORT — the liquid can cause nausea and diarrhea in some species.
steps.helpDetail: Fishhook barrel cactus is safer than compass barrel cactus.

techniques.name: Yucca Root Processing
techniques.subtitle: Starchy root vegetable of the desert

steps.instruction: Dig yucca root — thick, brown, radiating from the base.
steps.helpDetail: Dig yucca root — thick, brown, radiating from the base.

steps.instruction: Peel the tough outer bark to expose white starchy interior.
steps.helpDetail: Peel the tough outer bark to expose white starchy interior.

steps.instruction: Boil or roast until soft — raw yucca contains saponins that cause stomach upset.
steps.helpDetail: Boil or roast until soft — raw yucca contains saponins that cause stomach upset.

steps.instruction: High in carbohydrates, vitamin C, and potassium.
steps.helpDetail: Also produces natural soap when the root is crushed in water.

techniques.name: Prickly Pear Fruit Jelly
techniques.subtitle: Preserve cactus fruit for weeks

steps.instruction: Harvest ripe prickly pear fruit (bright red/purple).
steps.helpDetail: Harvest ripe prickly pear fruit (bright red/purple).

steps.instruction: Roll on ground to remove glochids, then peel.
steps.helpDetail: Roll on ground to remove glochids, then peel.

steps.instruction: Boil pulp with equal weight sugar for 30 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Boil pulp with equal weight sugar for 30 minutes.

steps.instruction: Strain through cloth for clear jelly, or leave pulp for fruit butter.
steps.helpDetail: Very high in antioxidants, vitamin C, and pectin (natural thickener). Stores for weeks.

techniques.name: Sassafras Root Tea
techniques.subtitle: Aromatic tea and flavoring from a common forest tree

steps.instruction: Identify: mitten-shaped, three-lobed, and simple oval leaves all on the same tree.
steps.helpDetail: Identify: mitten-shaped, three-lobed, and simple oval leaves all on the same tree.

steps.instruction: Dig small roots — scrub clean and slice thin.
steps.helpDetail: Dig small roots — scrub clean and slice thin.

steps.instruction: Steep in boiling water 10 minutes for aromatic tea.
steps.helpDetail: Steep in boiling water 10 minutes for aromatic tea.

steps.instruction: Traditional root beer flavoring.
steps.helpDetail: Also makes a thickening powder (filé) from dried leaves.

techniques.name: Spicebush Berry Harvest
techniques.subtitle: Wild allspice — aromatic seasoning for survival food

steps.instruction: Identify: aromatic shrub with small red berries in fall.
steps.helpDetail: Identify: aromatic shrub with small red berries in fall.

steps.instruction: Scratch stem — spicy smell confirms ID.
steps.helpDetail: Scratch stem — spicy smell confirms ID.

steps.instruction: Dry berries and grind as spice — tastes like allspice.
steps.helpDetail: Dry berries and grind as spice — tastes like allspice.

steps.instruction: Young leaves and twigs make aromatic tea.
steps.helpDetail: One of the most flavorful wild seasonings in eastern forests.

techniques.name: Wild Persimmon Harvest
techniques.subtitle: Ultra-sweet fruit available through early winter

steps.instruction: Identify: gray blocky bark, oval leaves, round orange fruit.
steps.helpDetail: Identify: gray blocky bark, oval leaves, round orange fruit.

steps.instruction: MUST be fully ripe (soft, wrinkled) — unripe is intensely astringent.
steps.helpDetail: MUST be fully ripe (soft, wrinkled) — unripe is intensely astringent.

steps.instruction: Eat raw when soft, or dry into leather.
steps.helpDetail: Eat raw when soft, or dry into leather.

steps.instruction: Very high in sugar (34%) when ripe — excellent energy food.
steps.helpDetail: Seeds can be roasted as coffee substitute.

techniques.name: Elderberry Harvest & Preparation
techniques.subtitle: Medicinal and nutritious berry — MUST be cooked

steps.instruction: Identify: compound leaves, flat-topped clusters of tiny purple-black berries.
steps.helpDetail: Identify: compound leaves, flat-topped clusters of tiny purple-black berries.

steps.instruction: COOK ALL elderberries — raw berries, leaves, and stems contain cyanogenic glycosides.
steps.helpDetail: COOK ALL elderberries — raw berries, leaves, and stems contain cyanogenic glycosides.

steps.instruction: Boil into syrup, jam, or wine.
steps.helpDetail: Boil into syrup, jam, or wine.

steps.instruction: Dried elderberry tea is antiviral — proven effective against influenza in clinical studies..
steps.helpDetail: Dried elderberry tea is antiviral — proven effective against influenza in clinical studies..

techniques.name: Sumac Lemonade
techniques.subtitle: Tart, refreshing wild drink from red sumac berries

steps.instruction: Identify STAGHORN SUMAC: fuzzy red berry clusters, compound leaves.
steps.helpDetail: Identify STAGHORN SUMAC: fuzzy red berry clusters, compound leaves.

steps.instruction: NOT poison sumac (white berries, swamp habitat).
steps.helpDetail: NOT poison sumac (white berries, swamp habitat).

steps.instruction: Crush berry clusters in cold water — soak 30 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Crush berry clusters in cold water — soak 30 minutes.

steps.instruction: Strain through cloth to remove fine hairs.
steps.helpDetail: Refreshing, tart, lemony drink high in vitamin C. Available late summer through winter.

techniques.name: Coconut Complete Utilization
techniques.subtitle: Water, meat, oil, fiber, shell — the ultimate tropical resource

steps.instruction: Green coconut: chop top for electrolyte-rich water (1 cup per nut).
steps.helpDetail: Green coconut: chop top for electrolyte-rich water (1 cup per nut).

steps.instruction: Mature coconut: crack open for dense white meat (350 cal per cup).
steps.helpDetail: Mature coconut: crack open for dense white meat (350 cal per cup).

steps.instruction: Grate and press meat for coconut oil and cream.
steps.helpDetail: Grate and press meat for coconut oil and cream.

steps.instruction: Husk fiber for cordage, shell for containers and charcoal.
steps.helpDetail: One tree provides food, water, building material, and fuel.

techniques.name: Breadfruit Cooking
techniques.subtitle: Starchy staple that feeds millions — cook like potato

steps.instruction: Harvest when mature (skin turns from green to yellow-green).
steps.helpDetail: Harvest when mature (skin turns from green to yellow-green).

steps.instruction: Roast whole over fire or in coals until skin chars and interior is soft.
steps.helpDetail: Roast whole over fire or in coals until skin chars and interior is soft.

steps.instruction: Peel and eat — texture like fresh bread, taste like potato.
steps.helpDetail: Peel and eat — texture like fresh bread, taste like potato.

steps.instruction: Can be sliced and dried for storage.
steps.helpDetail: One tree produces 200+ fruits per season.

techniques.name: Taro Root Preparation
techniques.subtitle: Tropical staple — MUST be cooked to remove toxins

steps.instruction: Harvest corms (bulb-like roots) from muddy areas near water.
steps.helpDetail: Harvest corms (bulb-like roots) from muddy areas near water.

steps.instruction: MUST BOIL 45+ minutes — raw taro contains calcium oxalate crystals that cause intense throat swelling.
steps.helpDetail: MUST BOIL 45+ minutes — raw taro contains calcium oxalate crystals that cause intense throat swelling.

steps.instruction: Peeled, boiled taro tastes like mild potato.
steps.helpDetail: Peeled, boiled taro tastes like mild potato.

steps.instruction: Leaves also edible when thoroughly boiled (change water twice).
steps.helpDetail: Major calorie source across tropical Pacific.

techniques.name: Banana Plant Complete Utilization
techniques.subtitle: More than just fruit — the entire plant is useful

steps.instruction: Fruit: eat ripe raw, cook green as starchy vegetable (like potato).
steps.helpDetail: Fruit: eat ripe raw, cook green as starchy vegetable (like potato).

steps.instruction: Flower: edible raw or cooked — popular in Southeast Asian cuisine.
steps.helpDetail: Flower: edible raw or cooked — popular in Southeast Asian cuisine.

steps.instruction: Leaves: waterproof wrappers for cooking, plates, temporary roofing.
steps.helpDetail: Leaves: waterproof wrappers for cooking, plates, temporary roofing.

steps.instruction: Trunk: contains drinkable water — chop and squeeze.
steps.helpDetail: Fiber: bark strips make strong cordage.

techniques.name: Nipa Palm Sugar Production
techniques.subtitle: Tap palm for sweet sap — tropical equivalent of maple syrup

steps.instruction: Cut the flower stalk and collect dripping sap in a container.
steps.helpDetail: Cut the flower stalk and collect dripping sap in a container.

steps.instruction: Fresh sap is sweet and drinkable — like coconut water.
steps.helpDetail: Fresh sap is sweet and drinkable — like coconut water.

steps.instruction: Boil to reduce into syrup or solid sugar blocks.
steps.helpDetail: Boil to reduce into syrup or solid sugar blocks.

steps.instruction: One palm produces 1-2 liters of sap per day for months.
steps.helpDetail: In the tropics, palm sugar is the most abundant sweetener available without agriculture.

techniques.name: Crowberry Foraging
techniques.subtitle: Abundant black berry of northern tundra and boreal forest

steps.instruction: Identify: low evergreen shrub with tiny needle-like leaves and black berries.
steps.helpDetail: Identify: low evergreen shrub with tiny needle-like leaves and black berries.

steps.instruction: Berries persist through winter under snow.
steps.helpDetail: Berries persist through winter under snow.

steps.instruction: Eat raw — mild, slightly bitter, not sweet.
steps.helpDetail: Eat raw — mild, slightly bitter, not sweet.

steps.instruction: Mix with rendered fat for pemmican — adds vitamins and moisture.
steps.helpDetail: One of the most abundant wild foods above the Arctic Circle.

techniques.name: Labrador Tea
techniques.subtitle: Warming tea from subarctic shrub — energy and morale boost

steps.instruction: Identify: leathery, rolled-edge leaves with fuzzy brown undersides.
steps.helpDetail: Identify: leathery, rolled-edge leaves with fuzzy brown undersides.

steps.instruction: Grows in bogs and boreal forests.
steps.helpDetail: Grows in bogs and boreal forests.

steps.instruction: Steep a few leaves in boiling water 5 minutes — aromatic, slightly resinous flavor.
steps.helpDetail: Steep a few leaves in boiling water 5 minutes — aromatic, slightly resinous flavor.

steps.instruction: Contains caffeine-like compounds.
steps.helpDetail: Do NOT over-steep or use large quantities — high doses cause GI upset.

techniques.name: Cloudberry Harvest
techniques.subtitle: Arctic golden raspberry — prized among the world's most expensive berries

steps.instruction: Identify: low plant with single white flower, one raspberry-like fruit per stem.
steps.helpDetail: Identify: low plant with single white flower, one raspberry-like fruit per stem.

steps.instruction: Ripens amber-gold in late summer on tundra and bogs.
steps.helpDetail: Ripens amber-gold in late summer on tundra and bogs.

steps.instruction: Eat raw — sweet, creamy, slightly tart.
steps.helpDetail: Eat raw — sweet, creamy, slightly tart.

steps.instruction: Extremely high in vitamin C (more than oranges).
steps.helpDetail: Traditionally preserved in animal fat. Considered a delicacy across Scandinavia.

techniques.name: Fireweed as Food & Tea
techniques.subtitle: First plant to colonize burned areas — tender greens and famous tea

steps.instruction: Identify: tall pink flower spikes on cleared or burned land.
steps.helpDetail: Identify: tall pink flower spikes on cleared or burned land.

steps.instruction: Young shoots: eat like asparagus (steam or boil 3 min).
steps.helpDetail: Young shoots: eat like asparagus (steam or boil 3 min).

steps.instruction: Mature leaves: dry for tea — this is traditional Russian Ivan Chai.
steps.helpDetail: Mature leaves: dry for tea — this is traditional Russian Ivan Chai.

steps.instruction: Stem pith: sweet and starchy, eat raw.
steps.helpDetail: Seed fluff: stuff into insulation or use as tinder.

techniques.name: Rock Tripe Lichen
techniques.subtitle: Emergency food found on rocks worldwide

steps.instruction: Identify: leathery, dark brown/black lichen growing flat on rocks.
steps.helpDetail: Identify: leathery, dark brown/black lichen growing flat on rocks.

steps.instruction: Peel from rocks — rubber-like when wet, brittle when dry.
steps.helpDetail: Peel from rocks — rubber-like when wet, brittle when dry.

steps.instruction: MUST soak in multiple changes of water, then boil 1+ hours to remove harsh acids.
steps.helpDetail: MUST soak in multiple changes of water, then boil 1+ hours to remove harsh acids.

steps.instruction: Minimal flavor, gummy texture, but prevents starvation.
steps.helpDetail: Explorer Sir John Franklin's men survived on this during Arctic expeditions.

techniques.name: Chestnut Roasting & Flour
techniques.subtitle: Sweet nutritious nut — must be cooked

steps.instruction: Identify: spiny husks containing 1-3 glossy brown nuts with pointed tips.
steps.helpDetail: Wild chestnut trees grow across eastern North America, Europe, and Asia.

steps.instruction: Score the flat side with an X cut through the shell.
steps.helpDetail: This prevents explosive bursting from steam buildup during roasting.

steps.instruction: Roast in coals for 15-20 minutes or boil for 30 minutes.
steps.helpDetail: Raw chestnuts contain tannins that cause stomach upset.

steps.instruction: Peel while warm. Eat directly or dry and grind into sweet, gluten-free flour.
steps.helpDetail: Chestnuts are 40% carbohydrate — more starchy than nutty. Major calorie source historically.

techniques.name: Crab Trapping (Coastal)
techniques.subtitle: High-protein coastal harvest with minimal gear

steps.instruction: Build a trap: weighted container with bait inside and funnel entrance.
steps.helpDetail: Crabs enter through the funnel to reach bait but cannot find their way back out.

steps.instruction: Bait with fish heads, chicken pieces, or any meat scraps.
steps.helpDetail: Strong-smelling bait attracts crabs from farther away.

steps.instruction: Set in shallow water along rocky coastline. Mark with a float or landmark.
steps.helpDetail: Low tide exposes trap for checking. High tide for optimal catch time.

steps.instruction: Boil crabs in seawater for 10-12 minutes. Crack shells and eat body and leg meat.
steps.helpDetail: One large crab provides 100+ calories of pure protein. Avoid crabs from polluted harbors.

techniques.name: Wild Turmeric Identification
techniques.subtitle: Anti-inflammatory root available in tropical regions

steps.instruction: Identify: large broad leaves emerging from ground, similar to ginger plant.
steps.helpDetail: Found in tropical forests and gardens. Break rhizome — bright yellow-orange inside.

steps.instruction: Harvest rhizome by digging around the base of the plant.
steps.helpDetail: The underground rhizome is the edible/medicinal part.

steps.instruction: Peel and grate raw, or dry and grind to powder.
steps.helpDetail: Raw turmeric stains everything yellow. Use gloves or sticks when handling.

steps.instruction: Add to food as seasoning or make tea for anti-inflammatory benefits.
steps.helpDetail: Curcumin (active compound) is a powerful anti-inflammatory. Combine with black pepper to increase absorption 2000%.

techniques.name: Trap Line Management Protocol
techniques.subtitle: Maximize trapping efficiency with systematic placement

steps.instruction: Set 10-20 traps along a 1-2 mile circuit that returns to camp.
steps.helpDetail: More traps = higher probability of catch. Vary trap types for different game.

steps.instruction: Check ALL traps at least twice daily: dawn and dusk.
steps.helpDetail: Unchecked traps cause animal suffering and allow predators to steal your catch.

steps.instruction: Move unproductive traps after 48 hours to new locations.
steps.helpDetail: If a trap hasn't caught anything in 2 days, the location is wrong.

steps.instruction: Keep a mental or physical log of catch rates by location and trap type.
steps.helpDetail: Data drives decisions: double down on productive locations, abandon unproductive ones. Systematic trapping outperforms random placement 5:1.

techniques.name: Fish Weir Construction
techniques.subtitle: Funnel fish into a catch basin using stone walls

steps.instruction: Build a V-shaped wall of stones across a stream, point downstream.
steps.helpDetail: The V funnels fish toward the narrow opening at the point.

steps.instruction: At the point of the V: create a small catch basin or cage.
steps.helpDetail: Fish enter the basin but the narrow opening makes exit difficult.

steps.instruction: Walls should extend above the water surface to prevent fish from jumping over.
steps.helpDetail: In shallow streams, a 12-inch wall is sufficient.

steps.instruction: Check the basin hourly during fish migration or after rain (when fish move more).
steps.helpDetail: Weirs are ancient technology — used for 10,000+ years. Once built, they catch fish indefinitely with no maintenance.

techniques.name: Land Snail Foraging
techniques.subtitle: Abundant protein source — available worldwide

steps.instruction: Collect snails after rain — they emerge in moist conditions.
steps.helpDetail: Garden snails, forest snails, and giant African snails are all edible.

steps.instruction: Purge for 48 hours in a container with clean vegetation or cornmeal.
steps.helpDetail: This clears their digestive tracts of potential toxins from plants they've eaten.

steps.instruction: Boil for 10 minutes, then pick from shells with a pin or twig.
steps.helpDetail: Must be cooked — raw snails carry parasites including rat lungworm.

steps.instruction: Season and eat. 80% protein by dry weight.
steps.helpDetail: Escargot is a delicacy for a reason. Rich in iron, magnesium, and phosphorus. Available in enormous quantities after warm rains.

techniques.name: Freshwater Mussel Harvest
techniques.subtitle: River protein available year-round

steps.instruction: Find mussels on river bottoms in clean-flowing water: feel with feet or hands.
steps.helpDetail: They partially bury themselves in gravel and sand. Look in water 1-3 feet deep.

steps.instruction: Harvest by pulling from substrate. Check regulations — some species are protected.
steps.helpDetail: Take only what you need. Do not harvest from stagnant or polluted water.

steps.instruction: Steam over fire or bake in coals until shells open (5-10 min).
steps.helpDetail: Discard any that DON'T open — they were dead before cooking and may be toxic.

steps.instruction: Eat the orange/cream colored meat inside. Chewy texture.
steps.helpDetail: Low calorie per mussel but available in large quantities. Good source of zinc, selenium, and B12.

techniques.name: Birch Sap to Syrup
techniques.subtitle: Concentrate birch sap into sweet syrup — boreal maple syrup equivalent

steps.instruction: Collect birch sap in spring: tap multiple trees for volume.
steps.helpDetail: You need 80-110 gallons of sap per gallon of syrup — more watery than maple.

steps.instruction: Boil in a wide, shallow pan over sustained fire.
steps.helpDetail: Wide pan = more surface area = faster evaporation. This takes hours.

steps.instruction: Continue until the liquid thickens and darkens.
steps.helpDetail: Birch syrup tastes different from maple — more complex, slightly savory.

steps.instruction: Store in sealed containers. Use as sweetener, medicine, or morale boost.
steps.helpDetail: Birch syrup contains xylitol (natural sugar alcohol) plus minerals. Highly valued in Nordic and Russian cultures.

techniques.name: Pinole (Parched Grain Flour)
techniques.subtitle: Ancient high-energy travel food from any grain or seed

steps.instruction: Parch raw seeds/grain in a dry hot pan or on a flat rock over fire.
steps.helpDetail: Toast until brown and fragrant. Stir constantly to prevent burning. Works with corn, wheat, wild seeds, amaranth.

steps.instruction: Grind toasted seeds into fine flour using stones.
steps.helpDetail: Mano and metate (flat stone base, cylindrical grinding stone) is the traditional method.

steps.instruction: Mix with water to drink, or eat dry as trail food.
steps.helpDetail: One tablespoon of pinole in a cup of water makes a filling, energizing drink.

steps.instruction: Pinole keeps indefinitely when dry. Ultra-light, ultra-compact.
steps.helpDetail: The Tarahumara runners consume pinole during 100+ mile ultramarathons. One of the most efficient trail foods ever created — more portable than pemmican.

techniques.name: Stinging Nettle Foraging
techniques.subtitle: Highly nutritious greens protected by formic acid

steps.instruction: Identify Urtica dioica in moist, rich soil.
steps.helpDetail: Square stem, opposite toothed leaves, covered in tiny hollow hairs (trichomes) containing formic acid and histamine. Brushing them causes immediate burning welts.

steps.instruction: Harvest using heavy gloves or improvising 'tongs' from two sticks.
steps.helpDetail: Harvest the top 4-6 inches of young plants before they flower (spring to early summer). Older plants develop gritty calcium carbonate particles (cystoliths) that irritate the kidneys.

steps.instruction: Neutralize the sting by boiling or crushing.
steps.helpDetail: Boiling the greens in water for 1-2 minutes completely dissolves the silica hairs, making them perfectly safe. Drying also neutralizes the sting.

steps.instruction: Consume as a nutrient-dense spinach substitute.
steps.helpDetail: Nettles are extraordinarily high in protein (up to 25% dry weight), iron, calcium, and vitamins A and C. Cooked nettle water serves as a nutrient-rich tea/broth.

techniques.sourceName: Peterson Field Guide to Edible Wild Plants

techniques.name: Cattail Shoot Harvest (Cossack Asparagus)
techniques.subtitle: Harvesting the inner core of the ultimate survival plant

steps.instruction: Locate Typha latifolia in shallow wetlands or ditches.
steps.helpDetail: Ensure the water source is not polluted (chemical runoff or raw sewage), as cattails are bioaccumulators and filter heavy metals from water.

steps.instruction: Grasp the inner core of leaves (the center spike) near the water line.
steps.helpDetail: Pull straight up firmly. The inner shoot will separate from the rhizome with a popping sound.

steps.instruction: Peel away the tough green outer layers.
steps.helpDetail: Continue peeling until you reach the crisp, white, tender core (usually the bottom 6-10 inches of the pulled stalk).

steps.instruction: Eat raw or boil briefly.
steps.helpDetail: Tastes similar to cucumber or zucchini. It provides a quick, easily digestible source of carbohydrates and vitamin K with zero toxic look-alikes if harvested from the center of mature cattail stands.

techniques.sourceName: Peterson Field Guide to Edible Wild Plants

techniques.name: Canine vs Feline Tracks
techniques.subtitle: Differentiating dog/wolf/coyote from bobcat/cougar tracks.

steps.instruction: Check for claw marks.
steps.helpDetail: Canines (dogs, coyotes, wolves, foxes) cannot retract their claws; claws almost always show in the dirt. Felines (cats, cougars) have retractable claws; prints rarely show claws unless leaping/running.

steps.instruction: Examine overall shape.
steps.helpDetail: Canine tracks are more oval-shaped and symmetrical. Feline tracks are wider, more round, and slightly asymmetrical (the 'leading toe' concept).

steps.instruction: Look at the heel pad (metacarpal pad).
steps.helpDetail: Canine heel pads have one lobe at the top and two at the bottom. Feline heel pads have two lobes at the top and three at the bottom (an 'M' shape).

steps.instruction: Draw the 'X'.
steps.helpDetail: In a canine track, you can draw a clean X through the negative space between the heel pad and toes without crossing the toe pads. You cannot do this on a feline track; the toes overlap.

techniques.name: Aging Ungulate (Deer) Tracks
techniques.subtitle: Determining how fresh a track is to gauge proximity to game.

steps.instruction: Assess edge sharpness.
steps.helpDetail: A fresh track (minutes to hours old) in soft dirt or snow will have perfectly vertical, crisp walls. As hours pass, wind, gravity, and sunlight round and degrade these sharp edges.

steps.instruction: Check for moisture and debris.
steps.helpDetail: A fresh track may expose dark, moist soil in the bottom that hasn't dried out in the sun yet. Any leaf or needle that falls inside the print means the animal passed before the debris fell.

steps.instruction: Examine the 'splash' or kick-up.
steps.helpDetail: When a hooved animal steps in mud, it kicks up a small rim around the track. If that rim is still wet and shiny, the track is extremely fresh.

steps.instruction: Compare with your own track.
steps.helpDetail: Step firmly next to the track. Compare the moisture, color, and edge quality of your brand new print to the animal's print. If they look identical, the animal is very close.

techniques.name: Spring-Pole Snare
techniques.subtitle: Lifting caught game off the ground to prevent predator theft.

steps.instruction: Select an incredibly supple sapling (the spring pole).
steps.helpDetail: Ash, hickory, or young maple work well. It must be strong enough to lift the target animal completely off the ground.

steps.instruction: Create the trigger mechanism (Toggle).
steps.helpDetail: Drive a stake into the ground near the trail. Carve a simple catch/notch in the stake. Tie a small toggle stick to the end of the spring pole rope.

steps.instruction: Bend the sapling and set the trigger.
steps.helpDetail: Bend the sapling down violently. Hook the toggle stick under the notch in the ground stake. The tension holds it in place, but an upward or forward pull releases the toggle.

steps.instruction: Set the wire noose.
steps.helpDetail: Run the snare wire from the toggle stick out over the game trail (head height for a rabbit is roughly 4 fingers off the ground). When the animal hits the noose, it pulls the toggle free, and the sapling snaps upright, hanging the catch.

techniques.name: Woven Fish Basket Trap
techniques.subtitle: Catching fish passively in flowing water.

steps.instruction: Gather extremely flexible green wood.
steps.helpDetail: Willow shoots, hazel, or thin bamboo are essential. You need dozens of long, straight withes.

steps.instruction: Weave the outer cone.
steps.helpDetail: Create a large, elongated cone (like a large vase). Tie the narrow end completely shut.

steps.instruction: Weave the inner funnel.
steps.helpDetail: Create a second, much smaller, shorter cone. Leave a hole at the narrow end just large enough for the target fish to squeeze through.

steps.instruction: Insert the funnel into the open end of the basket.
steps.helpDetail: The fish swim into the large opening, squeeze through the narrow hole at the end of the inner funnel, and are trapped in the main basket body (they cannot find the small hole to exit).

techniques.name: Foraging: True Morel
techniques.subtitle: Identifying Morchella species (The Foolproof Four).

steps.instruction: Locate old orchards or burn sites.
steps.helpDetail: Morels often fruit abundantly the spring following a forest fire, or under dying elm and apple trees.

steps.instruction: Examine the cap structure.
steps.helpDetail: A true morel cap looks like a deeply pitted honeycomb or sea sponge. The pits are indented into the flesh.

steps.instruction: Slice the mushroom in half lengthwise.
steps.helpDetail: This is the absolute diagnostic test. A TRUE morel is completely hollow from the tip of the cap down to the base of the stem. The cap is fully attached to the stem.

steps.instruction: Verify it is NOT a False Morel (Gyromitra).
steps.helpDetail: False morels have wavy, brain-like folds (not pits) and are NOT hollow inside (they are filled with cottony fibers). False morels contain monomethylhydrazine (rocket fuel) and are deadly.

techniques.sourceName: Peterson Field Guide to Edible Wild Plants

techniques.name: Foraging: Giant Puffball
techniques.subtitle: Identifying Calvatia gigantea (The Foolproof Four).

steps.instruction: Locate large, white spherical fungi.
steps.helpDetail: Found in meadows and deciduous forests in late summer/autumn. Sizes range from a baseball to a beach ball.

steps.instruction: Slice directly down the center.
steps.helpDetail: The inside MUST be pure, solid, marshmallow-white with zero outline of a stem or gills.

steps.instruction: Check for toxic lookalikes.
steps.helpDetail: If you see the faint outline of a mushroom shape (stem/gills) inside, it is a deadly Amanita 'egg' phase. DISCARD IT. If the inside is yellow, purple, or black, it's a toxic earthball (Scleroderma) or past its prime. DISCARD IT.

steps.instruction: Slice and fry.
steps.helpDetail: If pure white and solid throughout, slice into steaks and fry. Excellent protein source.

techniques.sourceName: Peterson Field Guide to Edible Wild Plants

techniques.name: Foraging: Chicken of the Woods
techniques.subtitle: Identifying Laetiporus sulphureus (The Foolproof Four).

steps.instruction: Locate dying or dead hardwood trees.
steps.helpDetail: Specifically oak. The mushroom grows in massive, overlapping, bracket-like shelves directly OUT of the tree trunk.

steps.instruction: Examine the color.
steps.helpDetail: Bright, almost neon orange on the top surface, and vivid sulfur-yellow on the underside pores.

steps.instruction: Check the underside.
steps.helpDetail: It must have tiny PORES underneath, NOT gills. Gills exclude it instantly.

steps.instruction: Harvest the outer edges.
steps.helpDetail: The outer 2 inches are tender and taste remarkably like chicken breast when cooked. The inner portions near the tree are woody and indigestible. DO NOT eat if growing on Pine, Yew, or Eucalyptus (absorbs toxins).

techniques.sourceName: Peterson Field Guide to Edible Wild Plants

techniques.name: Figure-4 Deadfall
techniques.subtitle: The classic mechanical advantage trigger for heavy weights.

steps.instruction: Carve the upward vertical post.
steps.helpDetail: Taper the top end to a flat chisel point. This is the foundation that holds the entire weight.

steps.instruction: Carve the horizontal bait stick.
steps.helpDetail: Cut a notch near the front to catch the vertical post, and taper the very front tip to rest against the diagonal stick. Smear peanut butter or fat on the far end.

steps.instruction: Carve the diagonal drop-stick.
steps.helpDetail: Cut a notch near the top to rest on the vertical post, and a notch near the bottom to catch the bait stick. This piece directly holds up the rock.

steps.instruction: Assemble under tension.
steps.helpDetail: The weight of the rock on the diagonal stick pinches the bait stick against the vertical post. When the animal bumps the bait stick, the friction lock breaks, and the entire structure collapses instantly.

techniques.name: Paiute Deadfall
techniques.subtitle: An ultra-sensitive string-trigger deadfall.

steps.instruction: Construct the Y-post and lever.
steps.helpDetail: Drive a Y-shaped stick into the ground. Lay a straight lever-stick across the Y. The rock rests on one end of the lever; a string hangs from the other end.

steps.instruction: Tie the toggle.
steps.helpDetail: Attach a small, 2-inch toggle stick to the bottom of the string hanging from the lever.

steps.instruction: Set the bait stick.
steps.helpDetail: Take a long, extremely thin bait stick. Pin the toggle against the vertical Y-post using the bait stick. The bait stick acts as a wedge.

steps.instruction: Bait the tip.
steps.helpDetail: Because the string holds the massive tension, the bait stick is under immense pressure but requires only ounces of sideways force to dislodge the toggle. It is 10x more sensitive than a Figure-4.

techniques.name: Promontory Peg Snare
techniques.subtitle: A toggle-release snare that lifts the catch instantly.

steps.instruction: Drive the peg and carve the notch.
steps.helpDetail: Drive a stake deep into the ground. Carve a distinct lip or shelf into the side of the peg facing the trail.

steps.instruction: Bend the sapling engine.
steps.helpDetail: Find a strong, flexible sapling nearby. Tie your main snare line to the top and bend it down viciously. Tie a small wooden toggle to the end of the line.

steps.instruction: Set the toggle under the peg lip.
steps.helpDetail: Hook the top edge of the toggle under the carved lip on the stake. The tension of the sapling pulls upward, locking it tightly in place.

steps.instruction: Run the snare loop.
steps.helpDetail: Run the actual noose loop out over the trail, attaching it firmly to the bottom of the toggle. When the animal hits the loop, it rips the toggle downward and off the lip, releasing the sapling.

articles.title: Caloric Needs in Survival
articles.body:
Your body needs fuel. Understanding caloric needs helps prioritize food sources.

Daily caloric needs:
- Resting in shelter: 1,200-1,500 cal
- Light activity: 2,000-2,500 cal
- Heavy activity (building, hiking): 3,000-4,000 cal
- Cold weather: add 500-1,000 cal for thermogenesis

Caloric density of survival foods:
- Fat: 9 cal/gram (most efficient)
- Protein: 4 cal/gram
- Carbohydrates: 4 cal/gram
- Insects: 400-500 cal per 100g
- Pine inner bark: 500-600 cal per 100g
- Pemmican: 250 cal per ounce

The body can survive 3 weeks without food but performance degrades rapidly after 3 days. Cognitive function and decision-making decline first — exactly when you need them most.

articles.title: Trapping vs. Hunting: Efficiency
articles.body:
Trapping is dramatically more efficient than hunting in survival situations.

Hunting:
- Requires weapon, skill, and energy expenditure
- You pursue ONE animal at a time
- Success rate for untrained hunters: ~5%
- Burns 300-600 calories per hour

Trapping:
- Set once, works 24/7
- Multiple traps = multiple chances simultaneously
- Set 12 snares for ~1 hr effort, catching odds improve to 50%+
- Burns 0 additional calories while waiting

Best practice: Set 10-12 snares on active game trails. Refresh every 48 hours. Check twice daily. The energy investment in setting traps pays dividends.

Ethical note: Check traps regularly. An animal suffering in a trap attracts predators to your area and wastes meat to stress hormones.

articles.title: Insects as Survival Food
articles.body:
Insects are the world's most underutilized food source. Over 2 billion people eat insects daily.

Nutrition per 100g:
- Crickets: 21g protein, 6g fat, 121 calories
- Grasshoppers: 20g protein, 6g fat, 153 calories
- Mealworms: 20g protein, 13g fat, 206 calories
- Termites: 14g protein, 2g fat, 535 calories
- Ants: 14g protein, 4g fat, 100 calories

Safe to eat (worldwide):
- Ants (not fire ants — remove heads), crickets, grasshoppers, beetle larvae/grubs, termites, cicadas, scorpions (remove stinger, cook)

Avoid:
- Brightly colored insects (warning coloration = toxic)
- Hairy caterpillars (urticating hairs cause allergic reactions)
- Anything that stinks when crushed
- Ticks, mosquitoes, flies (disease vectors)
- Spiders (not insects but commonly confused)

Collection methods: Turn over logs and rocks, shake vegetation in early morning when cold, attract with light at night.

articles.title: Fishing in Survival Situations
articles.body:
Fish are the easiest wild protein to catch with improvised gear.

Best times: Dawn and dusk. Fish feed most actively in low light.

Best locations:
- Where streams meet (confluences)
- Behind rocks in flowing water (fish rest in eddies)
- Undercut banks and overhanging vegetation
- Deeper pools in shallow streams
- Inlets and outlets of lakes

Passive fishing > active fishing. Set trotlines (multiple baited hooks on a line anchored at both ends) and fish traps, then go do other survival tasks.

Legal note: In survival situations, fish and game laws still technically apply. However, courts have consistently recognized 'necessity defense' for genuine survival situations.

Preservation: Fish spoils fastest of all meats. Cook immediately or smoke/dry within 2 hours of catching.

articles.title: Poisonous Plants: Warning Signs
articles.body:
When in doubt, do NOT eat it. These signs suggest a plant may be toxic:

- Milky or discolored sap (exception: dandelion)
- Beans, bulbs, or seeds inside pods
- Bitter or soapy taste
- Spines, thorns, or fine hairs
- 'Almond' scent in leaves or wood (= cyanide compounds)
- Three-leaflet growth pattern (poison ivy/oak)
- Umbrella-shaped flower clusters (hemlock family)
- White or yellow berries (90% toxic)
- Grain heads with pink, purple, or black spurs (ergot fungus)

Specific plants to memorize as deadly:
- Water hemlock (most violent plant toxin in North America)
- Death camas (looks like wild onion)
- Amanita mushrooms (death cap, destroying angel)
- Castor bean (contains ricin)
- Oleander (every part is lethal)
- Nightshade (berries look edible)

If plant poisoning occurs: induce vomiting if conscious and within 1 hour of ingestion. Drink large amounts of water. Seek medical care.

articles.sourceName: National Center for Home Food Preservation
articles.title: Food Preservation Without Refrigeration
articles.body:
In a survival situation, you cannot afford to waste food. Preservation methods:

Smoking (6-12 hours):
- Creates acidic surface that inhibits bacteria
- Adds antimicrobial compounds from wood smoke
- Result lasts 1-4 weeks

Drying/Dehydrating (1-3 days):
- Removes moisture bacteria need to grow
- Requires hot, dry, windy conditions
- Result lasts 2-4 weeks

Salting:
- Pack in salt or create a brine
- Draws moisture out through osmosis
- Result lasts months

Cold storage:
- Bury in stream beds (cold running water)
- Hang in shade at altitude (cool air)
- Cache in snow or permafrost

Fat rendering:
- Slowly heat fat until liquid
- Strain and store — pure rendered fat lasts months
- Used in pemmican production

Critical rule: when in doubt, smell it. Spoiled meat has a distinct sweet-putrid smell that's unmistakable. Trust your nose — it evolved specifically for this.

articles.title: Nutritional Priorities in Survival
articles.body:
In survival, calories matter more than balanced nutrition short-term.

Priority hierarchy:
1. Calories (energy to function)
2. Water (can't digest food without hydration)
3. Fat (most calorie-dense — 9 cal/gram)
4. Protein (muscle maintenance, immune function)
5. Carbohydrates (quick energy, brain fuel)
6. Micronutrients (Vitamin C prevents scurvy after 30 days)

Starvation timeline:
- Day 1-3: Glycogen depletion
- Day 3-7: Cognitive decline, irritability
- Day 7-14: Muscle breakdown for energy
- Day 14-21: Severe weakness, organ stress
- Day 21+: Cardiac irregularity risk

Performance declines severely after 72 hours. Even small amounts of food (insects, bark, roots) maintain cognitive function.

articles.title: Wild Plant Basics
articles.body:
Plants are the most accessible food source but also dangerous if misidentified.

Always-safe plants worldwide:
- Dandelion (entire plant edible)
- Cattail (shoots, roots, pollen year-round)
- Pine (inner bark, needles for tea, nuts)
- Clover (flowers and leaves)
- Plantain weed (leaves edible and medicinal)

Universal Edibility Test:
1. Test on skin (wait 15 min)
2. Touch to lip (wait 15 min)
3. Touch to tongue (wait 15 min)
4. Chew and spit (wait 15 min)
5. Eat small amount (wait 8 hours)

WARNING: This test does NOT work for mushrooms — some deadly mushrooms taste pleasant and show no symptoms for 6-12 hours before organ failure.

Cooking makes many marginal plants safer by breaking down irritants and toxins. When in doubt, boil it.

articles.title: Food Safety Without Refrigeration
articles.body:
Food poisoning in survival can be fatal — dehydration from vomiting depletes limited water reserves.

General rules:
- Cook ALL meat thoroughly (165°F/74°C)
- Eat food within 2 hours of cooking if not preserved
- Smell test: if it smells off, don't eat it
- Never eat meat from animals found already dead
- Avoid scavengers' kills (possible disease)

High-risk foods to avoid:
- Raw freshwater fish (parasites)
- Raw shellfish from warm waters
- Any plant with milky sap (except dandelion)
- Unidentified mushrooms
- Grain with black/purple growths (ergot fungus)

Safe handling:
- Clean hands before prep (ash works as soap)
- Separate raw meat from other foods
- Cook on clean surfaces
- Store surplus elevated and sealed

The survival paradox: you need food for energy, but contaminated food kills faster than starvation.

articles.title: Starvation Physiology: What Happens to Your Body
articles.body:
Understanding starvation helps you prioritize and conserve energy.

Phase 1 (0-24 hours): Glycogen depletion
- Body burns stored glycogen (sugar) in liver and muscles
- Blood sugar remains normal
- Performance is near normal
- Strong hunger signals

Phase 2 (1-3 days): Fat burning begins
- Body switches to burning fat for energy (ketosis)
- Hunger may actually decrease (ketones suppress appetite)
- Mental fog and irritability
- Fatigue during physical activity

Phase 3 (3-14 days): Sustained fat burning
- Body adapts to fat-burning metabolism
- Weight loss of 1-2 pounds per day
- Muscle wasting begins as body needs protein for vital functions
- Cold intolerance increases (less insulation, less metabolic heat)

Phase 4 (14+ days): Critical decline
- Muscle wasting accelerates
- Immune function collapses (infections become deadly)
- Mental function deteriorates severely
- Organ failure begins

Key takeaway: the body can survive 3+ weeks without food, but cognitive and physical performance degrades within days. In a survival situation, your brain failing to make good decisions is more dangerous than the starvation itself.

articles.title: Plant Poisoning: Recognition & Treatment
articles.body:
Misidentification of wild plants is the leading cause of foraging deaths.

Dangerous plant families:
- Hemlock (Conium, Cicuta): looks like carrot/parsley. Causes respiratory paralysis. Fatal.
- Death camas: looks like wild onion but NO onion smell. Causes heart failure.
- Nightshade: black or red berries. Neurological symptoms. Can be fatal.
- Castor bean: seeds contain ricin. A few chewed seeds can kill.
- Oleander: all parts toxic. Even smoke from burning is dangerous.

Universal warning signs in plants (avoid if present):
- Milky sap (exception: dandelion)
- Shiny leaves with thorns
- Seeds inside pods
- Bitter or soapy taste
- Umbrella-shaped flower clusters (many toxic species)

Poisoning treatment:
- If conscious: do NOT induce vomiting (may cause more damage)
- Activated charcoal if available (1g per kg body weight)
- Drink large amounts of water to dilute
- If convulsing: protect the head, maintain airway
- Red flag: any difficulty breathing, hallucinations, or loss of consciousness = critical emergency

Prevention rule: NEVER eat any plant you cannot identify with 100% certainty. The universal edibility test takes 24+ hours and should only be used as a last resort.

articles.title: Survival Protein Sources: Ranked by Effort
articles.body:
Not all protein sources are equal. Rank by calories gained versus calories expended to acquire them.

Best return on effort (passive methods):
1. Insects & grubs: everywhere, easy to collect, high calorie. Best ROI in survival.
2. Crayfish/shellfish: trap overnight, minimal effort, reliable.
3. Snares/traps: set and walk away. Check twice daily. 10+ traps = good odds.
4. Fishing (passive): set lines, trotlines, fish traps. Work while you sleep.

Moderate effort:
5. Fishing (active): hook and line, spearing. Hours of investment but reliable in good water.
6. Frogs: easy to catch by hand at night with a light source.
7. Birds (snares): passive but lower success rate than mammal snares.

High effort (often not worth it in short-term survival):
8. Large game hunting: requires weapon, skill, tracking. Very high energy expenditure.
9. Running down small game: exhausting and rarely successful.

Critical insight: a person who sets 12 snares and spends the day collecting insects and fishing with passive lines will outperform a person who spends all day stalking deer. The deer hunter exerts 3,000+ calories and may get nothing. The passive forager expends 500 calories and reliably gathers 1,000+.

Rule: work smarter, not harder. Passive calorie acquisition beats active hunting in survival.

articles.sourceName: National Center for Home Food Preservation
articles.title: Long-Term Food Preservation Methods
articles.body:
Surplus food spoils quickly. Preservation extends your food supply for days or weeks.

Drying/Dehydration:
- Most universal method. Works on meat, fish, plants, fruits, insects.
- Cut thin strips (1/4 inch). Hang in sun and wind, or smoke near fire.
- Properly dried food lasts weeks to months.
- Test: should be brittle, not bendy. Bendy = still moist = will rot.

Smoking:
- Smoke contains antimicrobial compounds that preserve meat.
- Cold smoking (below 90°F): best for preservation — 12-24 hours minimum.
- Hot smoking (above 150°F): cooks and partially preserves — eat within a week.
- Build a simple smokehouse: tripod frame, covered with bark or canvas.

Salt curing:
- Salt dehydrates bacteria. Pack meat in salt (if available).
- Salt + drying = most effective combination.
- Ocean water: boil to extract salt crystals.

Pemmican (ultimate survival food):
- Dried meat powder + rendered fat + dried berries.
- Lasts months to years if properly made.
- Calorie-dense: 135 cal per ounce.
- Complete nutrition: protein, fat, carbohydrates, vitamins.

Freezing (cold climates):
- Simply hang food above predator reach in freezing temperatures.
- Thaw and refreeze degrades quality — keep frozen solid.

articles.title: Primitive Cooking Methods
articles.body:
Cooking improves digestibility, kills pathogens, and makes many foods edible that are toxic raw.

Direct heat:
- Spit roasting: skewer food on stick over fire. Rotate constantly. Best for small game and fish.
- Planking: pin food to flat wood propped near fire. Great for fish fillets.
- Ember roasting: wrap food in green leaves, bury in hot coals. Root vegetables and tubers.

Boiling without a metal pot:
- Rock boiling: heat stones in fire, drop into water in bark/skin/stomach container. Water boils in minutes.
- Bamboo cooking: fill bamboo segment with water and food, prop over fire. Bamboo chars but doesn't burn through before food cooks.
- Clay pot: make a simple pot from clay (see technique).

Earth oven:
- Pit filled with hot rocks, food layered between wet leaves.
- Slow cooks large quantities — feeds groups.
- 4-8 hours cooking time.

Why cooking matters:
- Doubles the caloric yield from meat (cooked protein is 60% digestible vs 30% raw)
- Kills parasites (trichinella, tapeworm, toxoplasma)
- Breaks down plant toxins (tannins, oxalates, cyanogenic glycosides)
- Makes starchy roots digestible (raw starch passes through undigested)

Rule: always cook when possible. Raw food is a caloric and health gamble.

articles.title: Nutritional Deficiencies in Survival
articles.body:
Long-term survival requires more than just calories. Specific deficiencies create specific problems.

Vitamin C deficiency (Scurvy):
- Onset: 4-12 weeks without vitamin C
- Symptoms: bleeding gums, loose teeth, wounds won't heal, fatigue
- Sources: pine needle tea, rose hips, spruce tips, dandelion greens, berries
- Fix: any fresh plant material provides vitamin C

Salt deficiency (Hyponatremia):
- Risk: heavy sweating + drinking lots of water without salt
- Symptoms: nausea, headache, confusion, seizures
- Sources: sea water (boil to concentrate), animal blood, wood ash (contains potassium)

Fat deficiency (Rabbit starvation):
- Risk: eating only lean protein (rabbit, squirrel) without fat
- Symptoms: diarrhea, headache, fatigue, low blood pressure
- Your body CANNOT survive on protein alone — fat is essential
- Sources: bone marrow, brain tissue, grubs, nuts, fish oils

Iron deficiency:
- Risk: vegetarian survival diet, chronic blood loss
- Symptoms: weakness, rapid heartbeat, cold intolerance
- Sources: organ meat (liver), dark leafy greens, insects, cooking in iron vessels

Key insight: a varied diet solves most deficiency problems. Eat from every food group available: plants, meat, insects, fish. Organ meats are the most nutritionally complete single food source.
